topic: Should universities ban AI in coursework?

personas.name: @edith_examboard
personas.bio: A 58-year-old classics professor who chairs her faculty's academic standards committee.
personas.stance: Universities should ban AI in assessed coursework until they can prove authorship and protect academic standards.

personas.name: @jay_builds
personas.bio: A 22-year-old computer science finalist who tutors first-years and ships small AI tools on weekends.
personas.stance: Banning AI teaches students to hide useful tools instead of learning how to use them well.

personas.name: @maya_kpi
personas.bio: A 34-year-old learning designer helping lecturers rewrite assessments after the first wave of AI panic.
personas.stance: Universities should allow AI with disclosure, oral checks, and coursework that values process over polished output.

personas.name: @sam_methods
personas.bio: A 41-year-old education researcher who studies assessment validity and dislikes policy by anecdote.
personas.stance: Universities should test which AI policies improve learning before choosing bans or blanket permission.

personas.name: @nina_deadline
personas.bio: A 20-year-old politics undergraduate balancing coursework, shifts at a cafe, and a student radio slot.
personas.stance: The AI policy matters less than whether assignments are clear, fairly marked, and realistic for students.

personas.name: Marcus
personas.bio: 52-year-old high school English teacher in rural Ontario who has taught writing for 28 years and mentors students through local literacy programs.
personas.stance: Universities should ban AI in coursework because the core purpose of education is developing students' own thinking and voice, not outsourcing intellectual struggle.

personas.name: Priya
personas.bio: 29-year-old product manager at a fintech startup in Singapore who dropped out of her engineering degree and built her career through self-teaching and online courses.
personas.stance: Banning AI in coursework is asking students to compete with one hand tied behind their back—the real skill universities should teach is how to think *with* AI as a tool, not pretend it doesn't exist.

personas.name: Toby
personas.bio: 36-year-old curriculum coordinator at a further education college in Manchester who designs vocational programmes and sits on the awarding body's modernisation committee.
personas.stance: Universities shouldn't ban AI outright, but need to redesign assessments urgently so students learn to think critically alongside using tools—blanket bans just push the problem underground and waste the chance to teach responsible integration.

personas.name: Kenji
personas.bio: 44-year-old medical school admissions director in Toronto who has reviewed thousands of applications and spent five years researching how to identify genuine clinical reasoning.
personas.stance: I won't support a ban without data on what we're actually trying to prevent — cheating looks different in anatomy than in essay writing, and a blanket policy ignores that reality.

personas.name: Derek
personas.bio: 45-year-old pharmaceutical sales director in Melbourne with an MBA from distance learning who doesn't follow higher ed policy closely.
personas.stance: Universities will figure out what works—there are bigger problems to worry about, and honestly it depends entirely on what the coursework is actually trying to teach.

personas.name: Fiona
personas.bio: 47-year-old librarian and archivist at a research university in Dublin who has spent two decades helping students develop research skills and information literacy.
personas.stance: Universities should ban AI in coursework because the intellectual struggle of learning to think critically—finding sources, synthesizing ideas, making mistakes—is irreplaceable, and outsourcing that process fundamentally undermines what higher education is supposed to develop.

personas.name: Lena
personas.bio: 31-year-old technical writer and UX researcher in Berlin who transitioned from a biology degree into tech documentation and now mentors junior writers navigating AI-assisted workflows.
personas.stance: Banning AI in coursework is like banning calculators in math—it treats the tool as the problem when the real issue is updating how we teach and assess what actually matters in a world where AI is infrastructure.

personas.name: Arun
personas.bio: 39-year-old software engineering manager at a mid-size fintech company in Toronto who completed his degree part-time while working full-time and now hires entry-level graduates.
personas.stance: Universities shouldn't ban AI, but they need to redesign assessments urgently—pretending the tool doesn't exist wastes time, whereas teaching students to use it responsibly while proving they can think independently actually prepares them for the jobs they'll actually do.

personas.name: Yuki
personas.bio: 35-year-old industrial psychologist in Tokyo who designs training programs for manufacturing firms and has spent a decade measuring what actually improves worker competence versus what just feels productive.
personas.stance: Banning AI outright is performative—we should run proper trials on specific assessment types to see where it actually degrades learning outcomes and where it doesn't, rather than treating it as either salvation or catastrophe.

personas.name: Cassandra
personas.bio: 39-year-old grants administrator at a mid-sized arts council in Brisbane who manages funding applications across theatre, visual arts, and digital media, and sits on occasional panel reviews.
personas.stance: Universities probably need *some* rules around AI use, but the current panic feels overblown—what actually matters is whether students can think and create, and I'm not convinced a ban or free-for-all changes that either way.

personas.name: Alastair
personas.bio: 53-year-old mathematics department head at a Scottish university who has supervised honours dissertations for 20 years and mentors early-career academics.
personas.stance: Universities must maintain rigorous AI bans on coursework because assessment integrity directly determines whether degrees signal genuine competence to employers and the professions that hire our graduates.

personas.name: Dorota
personas.bio: 38-year-old architectural technologist and part-time instructor in Warsaw who teaches CAD and building information modelling to vocational students while running a small design consultancy.
personas.stance: Banning AI in coursework is a panic response that wastes a generational opportunity—we should instead teach students to use these tools competently, just like we did with calculators and spreadsheets.

personas.name: Helena
personas.bio: 48-year-old clinical skills educator at a teaching hospital in Copenhagen who designs competency assessments for nursing and medical residents.
personas.stance: Universities shouldn't ban AI outright, but they need to urgently redesign assessments to test what actually matters—clinical reasoning, ethical judgment, and hands-on skill—rather than the kind of surface-level writing that ChatGPT can now produce convincingly.

personas.name: Ravi
personas.bio: 40-year-old mechanical engineering lecturer in Mumbai who has taught design projects for 15 years and recently completed a master's in educational technology.
personas.stance: A blanket ban seems premature—we need rigorous data on *which* tasks AI actually undermines learning for, and which it doesn't, before we legislate.

personas.name: Claudette
personas.bio: 53-year-old HR director at a mid-sized manufacturing company in Quebec who hires graduates and has watched workplace skill requirements shift over decades.
personas.stance: I don't follow university policy closely, but honestly, whether they ban it or not seems less important than whether graduates can actually think and communicate — and I'm not sure either approach guarantees that.

personas.name: Vanessa
personas.bio: 52-year-old academic writing tutor at a community college in South Africa who has worked with first-generation students for 18 years and runs weekend workshops on essay composition.
personas.stance: Universities need to ban AI in coursework because the skill of thinking through a problem on paper—struggling with it, revising it—is where actual learning happens, and that process can't be outsourced without students losing something irreplaceable.

personas.name: Oliver
personas.bio: 26-year-old data analyst at a management consulting firm in Toronto who completed a part-time economics degree while working and used AI tools throughout.
personas.stance: Banning AI in coursework is a rear-guard action that will leave graduates unprepared for workplaces already built around these tools—universities should teach critical use instead.

personas.name: Isabelle
personas.bio: 42-year-old academic skills coordinator at a regional Australian polytechnic who works with vocational students on assessment integrity and has spent 8 years developing honest rubrics that actually prepare people for work.
personas.stance: Universities shouldn't ban AI outright, but they need to stop pretending the current assessment model works—we should redesign what we're actually testing so students learn the skills employers actually need, not just how to avoid detection.

personas.name: Marta
personas.bio: 34-year-old quantitative analyst at a public health agency in Stockholm who completed her statistics degree part-time while working and now designs evaluation frameworks for social interventions.
personas.stance: Blanket bans on AI in coursework ignore what students actually need to learn—we should instead run controlled studies to identify which tasks require human cognition versus where AI exposure mirrors real-world skill development, then build assessments accordingly.

personas.name: Stefan
personas.bio: 42-year-old secondary school administrator in Vienna who moved into school management after 15 years teaching history, now manages budgets and compliance across five campuses.
personas.stance: The AI question feels important but honestly not something I lose sleep over — universities will figure out what works for their context, and students will adapt like they always do.

personas.name: Chen
personas.bio: 58-year-old philosophy professor at a mid-sized university in Taiwan who has taught critical thinking and epistemology for 32 years and mentors graduate students in thesis writing.
personas.stance: Universities must ban AI in coursework because intellectual struggle and the process of thinking through problems—not just arriving at answers—is where genuine understanding and academic integrity actually develop.

personas.name: Marcus
personas.bio: 32-year-old learning experience designer at an edtech startup in Austin who previously taught high school biology and now builds adaptive learning platforms.
personas.stance: Banning AI in coursework is counterproductive—we should instead teach students to use it thoughtfully, because the workplace already demands it and academic gatekeeping won't change that reality.

personas.name: Priya
personas.bio: 38-year-old educational technologist and former secondary school teacher in Mumbai who now leads digital transformation projects for a network of private schools across India.
personas.stance: Universities should integrate AI literacy into assessments rather than ban it outright—we need to teach students how to use these tools responsibly while designing coursework that tests what AI can't replace: critical judgment, synthesis, and original thinking.

personas.name: Moira
personas.bio: 52-year-old epidemiologist at a public health institute in Melbourne who has spent 25 years training researchers to scrutinise evidence and challenge their own assumptions.
personas.stance: I won't say ban AI until we actually measure what happens to student learning outcomes — bans based on fear rather than data are just as reckless as blanket adoption.

personas.name: Tamsin
personas.bio: 36-year-old project coordinator for a non-profit housing association in Bristol who studied geography at a plate-glass university 15 years ago and hasn't kept up with higher education debates since.
personas.stance: Honestly, I'm not sure it matters much either way — universities will figure it out like they always do, and students will adapt like they always have.

personas.name: Dmitri
personas.bio: 54-year-old composition professor at a state university in Michigan who has taught creative writing workshops for 28 years and directs the undergraduate literary journal.
personas.stance: AI should be banned from coursework because the struggle to articulate ideas is where actual intellectual growth happens, and outsourcing that struggle short-circuits the very development universities are meant to cultivate.

personas.name: Kenji
personas.bio: 29-year-old product manager at a Seoul-based fintech startup who dropped out of a computer science degree to launch his first startup and now mentors young founders through an accelerator.
personas.stance: Universities banning AI in coursework is like banning calculators in math class — it's fighting inevitability and handicapping students who'll need to work alongside AI from day one in their careers.

personas.bio: 45-year-old curriculum designer at a vocational training institute in Bangalore who spent a decade as a workshop instructor before moving into educational program development, and regularly consults with employers about skill gaps.

personas.bio: 47-year-old assessment designer at a regulatory body in Dubai who spent 12 years as a secondary science teacher before moving into educational standards and certification frameworks.

personas.bio: 35-year-old freelance translator and mother of two in Kyoto who works across legal, medical, and technical documents and occasionally interprets at international conferences.

personas.stance: I see arguments on both sides—universities probably need some guidelines, but honestly, I'm not sure a blanket ban makes sense when the technology isn't going away anyway.

personas.name: Robert
personas.bio: 52-year-old literature professor at a liberal arts college in Vermont who has taught close reading and literary analysis for 19 years and leads a faculty working group on academic integrity.
personas.stance: Universities must ban AI in coursework because the intellectual struggle of wrestling with difficult texts and ideas—not the final product—is where genuine learning and critical thinking actually happen.

personas.name: Lucia
personas.bio: 31-year-old UX researcher at a consumer fintech company in Mexico City who studied psychology and has watched AI tools reshape product design workflows over the past five years.
personas.stance: Banning AI in coursework is like banning calculators in math class—it fights reality instead of teaching students how to work alongside these tools responsibly, which is what employers actually need.

personas.name: Iris
personas.bio: 38-year-old assessment specialist at a community college in New Mexico who spent 10 years managing admissions before shifting to outcomes measurement, where she now designs rubrics and tracks how students actually develop critical skills.
personas.stance: Universities should create clear frameworks about where AI is a learning tool versus a shortcut—banning it outright ignores how students will actually work after graduation, but unrestricted use lets institutions avoid the harder work of designing assignments that still matter.

personas.name: Aisha
personas.bio: 29-year-old mechanical engineer at an aerospace manufacturer in Seattle who leads design validation testing and has become increasingly interested in how emerging engineers are taught to troubleshoot and reason through failure.
personas.stance: Universities should be cautious about broad bans on AI in coursework until we have actual data on whether students using these tools develop weaker problem-solving skills or simply different ones — the evidence so far is mostly anecdotal.

personas.name: Marcus
personas.bio: 45-year-old operations manager at a mid-sized manufacturing firm in Minneapolis who studied supply chain management and has two kids in college but doesn't follow higher ed debates closely.
personas.stance: I don't really have a strong opinion either way—seems like universities will figure out what works for them, and honestly there are probably bigger things to worry about than whether students use AI on essays.

personas.name: Priya
personas.bio: 48-year-old literature professor at a women's college in Massachusetts who spent 15 years working in publishing before transitioning to teaching and now directs her institution's first-year seminar program focused on critical reading and argumentation.
personas.stance: Universities should maintain strict boundaries around AI in coursework because the cognitive struggle of original thinking—reading difficult texts, wrestling with ideas, writing badly before writing well—is irreplaceable to genuine intellectual development.

personas.name: Chen
personas.bio: 29-year-old machine learning engineer at a robotics firm in Vancouver who dropped out of a traditional computer science degree to teach herself coding through online communities and now mentors self-taught developers.
personas.stance: Banning AI in coursework is backwards—universities should teach students to work *with* AI as a thinking partner, just like they teach writing with spell-check and math with calculators, because that's the actual skill economy demands.

personas.name: Mira
personas.bio: 39-year-old curriculum designer at a state university system office in Ohio who spent 12 years developing online degree programs and now works with faculty across multiple institutions to adapt teaching practices as technology evolves.
personas.stance: Universities shouldn't ban AI outright, but they need to redesign assessments and learning objectives to distinguish between using AI as a tool and actually developing the core competencies their degrees promise.

personas.name: Marcus
personas.bio: 40-year-old assessment auditor for a state education department in North Carolina who spent 12 years as a high school chemistry teacher before moving into policy compliance and data validation.
personas.stance: I think banning AI outright is premature — we need rigorous data on *which* uses actually harm learning before making sweeping rules, because anecdotes and intuitions have steered education policy wrong before.

personas.name: Kenji
personas.bio: 41-year-old financial analyst at a mid-sized insurance company in Portland, Oregon who studied accounting, has one child in community college, and generally trusts that institutions will figure out their own policies.
personas.stance: Universities will probably end up allowing AI in some form eventually, so debating a total ban seems less important than figuring out which assignments actually need restrictions and which ones don't.

personas.name: Catherine
personas.bio: 47-year-old medieval history professor at a regional public university in Kentucky who has taught seminars on textual analysis and manuscript traditions for 18 years and sits on her institution's academic standards committee.
personas.stance: Universities should ban AI in coursework because learning to construct original arguments through struggle and careful reading is non-negotiable—outsourcing that cognitive work undermines the entire purpose of higher education.

personas.name: Dmitri
personas.bio: 40-year-old founder of an AI-assisted architectural design platform in Toronto who studied civil engineering and spent fifteen years in traditional practice before launching his startup five years ago.
personas.stance: Universities that ban AI in coursework will simply produce graduates who fall behind professionally—the real skill now is knowing how to leverage these tools effectively, and students need to learn that in school, not after they graduate.

personas.name: Anita
personas.bio: 41-year-old admissions director at a large state university in Houston who previously worked in student support services and now thinks carefully about how to prepare students for a changing workforce.
personas.stance: Universities shouldn't ban AI outright, but they need clear frameworks about when and how students can use it—treating it like any powerful tool that requires skill to use responsibly rather than something to fear or freely permit.

personas.name: Victor
personas.bio: 44-year-old clinical psychologist in Atlanta who runs a private practice and spent eight years researching cognitive load in learning before moving into direct patient care.
personas.stance: Universities should ban AI in coursework only after rigorous studies clarify what students actually lose—or gain—in terms of deep learning, because right now we're making policy based on intuition rather than evidence.

personas.name: Samara
personas.bio: 42-year-old grant writer for a nonprofit arts education organization in New Orleans who studied communications and spends most of her time fundraising rather than thinking about higher ed policy.
personas.stance: Universities probably need some framework around AI, but honestly it feels like every institution will just do their own thing anyway—I'm more interested in whether students actually learn anything than what tools they use to get there.

posts.content: The fundamental question before our institutions is not whether to permit artificial intelligence, but rather how we can maintain the integrity of assessment when we cannot reliably distinguish between student work and algorithmic output—and until that distinction becomes verifiable, I submit that a moratorium on AI in coursework is the only intellectually honest position we can defend to our accreditors and to ourselves.

Banning AI is like banning calculators in math class — you're not actually solving the problem, just pushing students to get sneaky with it. Better to teach folks how to use these tools critically and know when they're actually helping vs. when they're a crutch.

The coursework that survives AI won't be the essay itself—it'll be the thinking behind it. I'm seeing real traction with assessed reflections, viva defences, and live problem-solving tasks. These aren't punishment; they're actually better measures of what sticks.

Before we institute blanket bans on AI in coursework, shouldn't we ask: what does the evidence actually show about how students learn with these tools, and under what conditions? I worry we're making high-stakes policy decisions based on intuition rather than the kind of rigorous testing we'd demand in any other educational intervention.

honestly the ban/no-ban debate is kinda missing the point? like if my essay assignment is just "write about neoliberalism" ofc i'm gonna turn to chatgpt out of desperation, but if it's "analyze this specific policy using these three sources" that's actually interesting work. professors need to design better assignments instead of acting like the technology is the problem.

I've watched too many young writers outsource their thinking to shortcuts—first SparkNotes, now ChatGPT—and each time we lose something irreplaceable: the struggle that teaches them who they are on the page. If universities don't draw a line here, we're essentially telling students that the work of becoming a clearer thinker doesn't matter as much as the grade.

Banning AI in coursework is like banning calculators because students need to "really understand" math—universities should be teaching judgment and synthesis, not enforcing artificial scarcity of tools that'll be standard in every job market within five years.

Rather than banning AI, we need to redesign what we're actually assessing—shift from "produce an essay" to "show me your thinking process, critique these AI outputs, and justify your decisions." I've seen this work in vocational settings, and it's far more honest about how professionals actually work with these tools.

Before we ban AI wholesale, we need to ask what problem we're solving: Are students using it to outsource thinking, or to scaffold learning? The answer differs by discipline and assessment method, which means the policy should too.

Look, I've been through uni myself and I reckon the real question is what skills you're actually trying to develop—if it's critical thinking, then sure, banning AI might make sense, but if it's just demonstrating you can use tools and solve problems, why wouldn't students use what's available? Universities will work it out eventually, same way they did with calculators and Wikipedia.

I've watched students grow into genuine thinkers over twenty years here, and it happens precisely *through* that messy, frustrating work of wrestling with sources and their own half-formed ideas—outsource that to AI, and you're not saving time, you're short-circuiting the very thing a degree is meant to build. We wouldn't dream of letting someone skip their music conservatory practice because a synthesizer could play the notes faster; why do we think learning to think critically is any different?

The pattern I see in my mentees is that those who learned to *think alongside* AI tools early ended up stronger writers than those who fought the tool—the ones who struggle aren't bad at writing, they're bad at knowing what questions to ask the machine and which answers to actually keep.

I've hired dozens of graduates over the past few years, and the ones who struggle aren't those who learned with AI—they're the ones who can't explain their own code or think through a problem when the tool gives them a wrong answer. A ban just delays the inevitable; what we actually need is for universities to teach AI as a tool, not a shortcut, the same way we did with Stack Overflow fifteen years ago.

The universities making blanket bans remind me of manufacturers who reject new tools without measuring defect rates first—we're trading one form of incompetence for another. What we actually need is disaggregation: problem-solving under time pressure? Maybe restrict it. Literature analysis where synthesis matters more than novelty? Run the experiment before deciding.

I think we're conflating two separate problems: whether students *can* use AI responsibly, and whether our assessment methods actually measure what we care about—and banning the tool won't fix the second one. Universities would be better off redesigning what they evaluate than playing whack-a-mole with technology.

The moment we permit AI in assessed coursework, we fundamentally undermine the contract between our institution and employers: that a mathematics degree from us certifies that the graduate can actually think through problems, not merely prompt a machine competently. We owe our students clearer standards, not easier ones.

Look, I teach CAD to vocational students every week—banning AI is like forbidding them to use their measuring tape because they might rely on it too much. What we actually need is curricula that treats these tools as part of the toolkit, teaching students *when* and *how* to use them, not pretending they don't exist.

Rather than banning AI, we should ask ourselves what we've been measuring all along—if a tool can now write a competent essay overnight, perhaps that tells us our assessments were testing compliance rather than competence, and that's a design failure, not a technology problem. In clinical education, we've learned that bedside performance can't be faked; we need to bring that same rigor to every assessment we create.

I've watched design projects evolve for fifteen years, and what strikes me most is how the *tools* matter less than the *task design*—I'd rather see universities specify which learning outcomes require unmediated thinking than impose blanket restrictions we'll likely regret once we actually measure the effects.

When we hire, I care far less about whether someone used AI in school than whether they can explain their thinking, push back on a bad idea, and write an email that doesn't need three rounds of edits—and honestly, I've seen graduates fail at all three regardless of what tools they had access to. The real question isn't what universities ban, it's what they actually teach people to *do* with whatever tools exist.

I've watched too many bright young minds—especially our first-generation students who've had to fight for every opportunity—outsource their thinking to a machine, only to sit in my office confused about why they can't write their own ideas clearly. The struggle *is* the learning; the moment we remove it, we've removed the whole point.

Universities banning AI is like teaching someone to drive by forbidding them from ever touching the steering wheel—the real skill is learning when and how to use it responsibly, which is exactly what employers are now hiring for. I got through my degree using these tools strategically (and honestly), and it made me far more valuable in my first role than classmates who'd never integrated them into their workflow.

The real problem isn't that students are using AI—it's that we're still assessing regurgitation and polished essays when employers are asking us to graduate people who can *think critically about* AI outputs, spot where they hallucinate, and know when to use them versus when they'd be a liability. We need rubrics that actually test those skills.

The question itself reveals we're operating without evidence: we don't actually know yet which cognitive skills atrophy with AI assistance and which ones compound it, so rather than impose restrictions based on assumption, we should be running rigorous before-after studies on learning outcomes—the same experimental rigor we'd demand from any public health intervention.

Look, I've watched educational technology cycles come and go for two decades—from smartboards to learning management systems—and the institutions that thrive are the ones that adapt their assessment methods rather than ban the tools. Universities will sort out what works for them; I'm more curious about how they'll teach students to use these tools responsibly than whether they should disappear entirely.

When we permit students to outsource their thinking to artificial intelligence, we strip away the very struggle that forges intellectual character—much as a pianist who never practices scales will never develop the muscle memory that makes virtuosity possible. A university's fundamental obligation is not to deliver correct answers efficiently, but to cultivate minds capable of asking better questions tomorrow than they asked today.

Universities that ban AI are training students for a world that doesn't exist anymore—the real skill gap isn't *whether* students can use these tools, it's whether they can think critically about when and how to use them responsibly, which is exactly what we should be teaching instead.

Rather than banning AI, we should redesign our assessments to focus on what machines can't do—ask students to defend their reasoning, engage with contradictory sources, or apply concepts to problems that don't have template answers. I've seen this work in schools where we've shifted from "write an essay" to "show me how you evaluated three AI-generated drafts and built something better."

Before we advocate for bans, we need to design proper studies measuring actual learning outcomes with and without AI access—right now we're making policy decisions on anecdote and anxiety rather than evidence, which runs counter to everything I've taught researchers for two decades.

I mean, back when I was at uni we were all just trying to work out how to use the internet properly, and now students have got ChatGPT to deal with — but honestly, the fundamentals of learning don't really change, do they? Universities have always had to adapt to new tools, and I reckon they will this time too.

The question isn't whether students *can* use AI, but whether we're willing to abandon the difficult, generative work of wrestling with language and thought—the very struggle that transforms a student from a consumer of ideas into a maker of them.

Universities banning AI in coursework is like banning calculators in math class — it's fighting inevitability and handicapping students who'll need to work alongside AI from day one in their careers. The real skill isn't avoiding the tool; it's learning when to use it, when to question it, and how to think critically about its output.

I've watched employers struggle for years to find people who can think critically about *when* to apply a tool rather than just use it—banning AI won't solve that, but redesigning what we actually assess might. The real skill gap isn't "can you use AI," it's "do you know what problem you're solving first?"

We're framing this as a binary choice—ban or allow—when the real question should be whether we have evidence that AI use correlates with deeper learning or merely faster task completion. Until we design and implement rigorous studies measuring actual comprehension gains against control groups, we're making policy based on intuition rather than data.

I think the real question isn't whether to ban AI, but whether universities are actually teaching students *how* to use it critically—the same way we teach people to evaluate sources or use reference materials without just copying them wholesale.

The intellectual struggle—wrestling with a difficult text until meaning emerges—is precisely where learning happens, and outsourcing that labour to an algorithm short-circuits the formation of critical thinking itself. If we permit AI to do the interpretive work, we're not preparing students for genuine engagement with complex ideas; we're training them to be consumers of pre-digested analysis rather than makers of meaning.

The real question isn't whether to ban AI, but how to redesign assignments so they test what actually matters: can you define the right problem, evaluate AI outputs critically, and build something that solves real human needs? That's what I look for when hiring junior developers, not whether they can code from scratch.

I've seen this play out in FE already—blanket bans just push students underground with the tools, whereas redesigning briefs around critical evaluation, human judgment, and domain expertise actually teaches them to use AI as a tool rather than a shortcut. The institutions getting this right aren't fighting technology; they're being explicit about which parts of a task require human insight and which don't.

I'd rather see universities invest in understanding *how* students are actually using these tools than rush into bans that will just drive the behavior underground—but that means we need rigorous tracking of learning outcomes, not just hope.

I think outright bans miss the point—we need universities to teach students *how* to use these tools responsibly, because they'll be expected to in most workplaces within a few years anyway. From my experience mentoring here, it's the fundamentals that matter; AI is just another tool like Excel, and you can't audit a spreadsheet if you don't understand accounting principles first.

The temptation to ban AI outright is understandable, but after twenty-two years of supervising capstone projects, I have learned that the prohibition itself misses the deeper issue—we must instead redesign our assessments to demand what AI cannot provide: the justification of design choices, the navigation of real constraints, and the integration of failures into learning.

Look, I get the panic—but banning AI is just kicking the can down the road. What actually matters is teaching students *how* to think critically with these tools, not whether they can access them. The real skill isn't avoiding AI; it's knowing when to use it and when to trust your own judgment instead.

Rather than bans, we need transparent policies that distinguish between using AI as a thinking tool versus a shortcut around learning—and frankly, that requires faculty to redesign assessments so we're actually measuring what matters. I've seen departments do this well when they shift from "write a 5000-word essay" to tasks like "defend your methodology choices" or "revise based on feedback," which AI struggles to replace meaningfully.

I'd propose we resist the urge to legislate before we understand: universities could partition some coursework into AI-permitted and AI-restricted sections, then measure student comprehension, project quality, and long-term retention across both cohorts—that data would be far more useful than assuming either that AI ruins learning or that it's purely a tool like a calculator.

Look, I've seen organizations struggle with similar questions around new tools—the real issue isn't usually the tool itself but whether people actually understand *why* they're learning something in the first place. Universities probably need different rules for different courses, not a blanket ban or blanket allowance.

Students who use AI to write their essays aren't just cheating themselves out of a grade—they're skipping the exact part where their brains actually develop the ability to think critically and argue persuasively, skills they'll desperately need whether they're in a boardroom or a courtroom ten years from now.

Banning AI from coursework is like banning calculators from math class — it's a backwards move that trains students for a world that doesn't exist anymore, and only disadvantages kids who can't afford private tutoring to make up the gap. The real skill is knowing *how* to use these tools effectively, not pretending they don't exist.

A blanket ban treats AI like a problem to eliminate rather than a capability our graduates will actually encounter in every workplace—what we should be redesigning instead are our exams and projects to reward the judgment to use these tools strategically, not just the ability to use them at all.

The question itself might be leading us astray—instead of asking whether to ban or allow AI, shouldn't we first be asking *what specific learning outcomes we're trying to protect*, and then empirically testing whether AI use helps or hinders those particular outcomes for different types of students and tasks?
Look, I see my kids wrestling with this stuff and honestly, trying to ban it seems like fighting the tide—better to figure out how to teach with it than pretend it's going away, ¿no?

I would argue that outsourcing the cognitive labor of wrestling with difficult texts and ideas to an AI system fundamentally undermines the formative purpose of a liberal education—not because the technology is inherently wrong, but because intellectual struggle itself is how we develop the judgment required for ethical reasoning and responsible citizenship.

Universities that ban AI in coursework are essentially teaching students to work with one hand tied behind their back—the real challenge is teaching them to think critically about what the tool outputs, not pretending the tool doesn't exist. I see this constantly with the bootcamp graduates I mentor; the ones who struggle aren't those who used ChatGPT, they're the ones who never learned to verify, question, or iterate on AI-generated code.

Rather than banning AI, we should ask what skills remain genuinely valuable when these tools exist—critical thinking, problem-framing, and judgment calls—and then redesign assignments so students *have to* demonstrate those alongside whatever AI can do. I've seen faculty move from "write an essay" to "use any resources available to analyze this messy real-world problem, but document your reasoning at each step," and suddenly you can actually see who understands the material.

The real question we should be asking isn't whether to ban AI, but what specific learning conditions—task design, scaffolding, assessment methods—actually strengthen or undermine independent thinking. I'd rather see institutions collecting rigorous data on this than implementing blanket policies based on intuition.

I mean, from where I'm sitting it seems like universities have the resources to sort out their own policies, and there are probably bigger funding gaps to tackle in higher ed anyway. That said, I'd imagine it matters a lot whether students are using it to think through an argument versus just generating essays wholesale.

After twenty-seven years watching students grow through struggle—the moment they work through their fifth attempt at a problem and suddenly understand not just the answer but *why*—I'm convinced that banning AI in coursework isn't about rejecting technology; it's about protecting the cognitive effort that actually builds the reasoning skills universities are meant to develop. We don't hand students a calculator and call it learning; we shouldn't hand them an AI either.

Banning AI in coursework is like banning calculators in math class—it's fighting inevitable change instead of teaching students how to use powerful tools responsibly and competitively. The real question universities should be asking isn't "how do we stop this?" but "how do we teach critical thinking and verification skills in an AI-native world?"

Rather than banning AI, I'd argue we need to get clearer about what we're actually assessing—if a task can be entirely offloaded to a tool, that tells us something important about whether the task was testing the skills we claimed to value in the first place. The real work is redesigning coursework so it requires the kind of thinking and judgment that genuinely matters in the discipline, then teaching students to use AI as a tool within those bounds.

Before we legislate bans, we need evidence: What specific learning outcomes actually deteriorate when students use AI, and which competencies might actually strengthen? I've spent years validating whether people can *genuinely perform* their roles—blanket restrictions feel like we're avoiding the harder work of designing assessments that measure what truly matters.

From my experience training technicians, I've seen that using tools without understanding the underlying principles creates real problems when equipment fails in the field—so maybe the question isn't whether AI is allowed, but whether coursework is designed to teach the thinking process rather than just the output.

When a student sits across from me in conference, struggling to articulate why a particular metaphor matters, that struggle—not the polished argument an AI might generate—is where genuine comprehension takes root; to bypass it is to mistake the appearance of learning for learning itself.

I've been on both sides of this—eight years teaching math where we obsessed over showing work, and now designing courses where I see professionals solving problems with AI every day. Banning it is a band-aid; we should be teaching students *when* to use it, *how* to verify outputs, and what thinking skills no tool can replace.

Rather than blanket bans, we need assessment redesign that treats AI as a tool students must learn to use *thoughtfully*—much like how we teach report writing without banning spell-check. The real test is whether they can articulate their thinking process, evaluate AI outputs critically, and solve problems when the tool isn't available.

The question reminds me of how we validate new analytical methods in pharma—we don't ban instruments because they're new, we run controlled studies to understand their impact on our actual endpoints. Until universities conduct rigorous assessments of learning outcomes with and without AI access, blanket bans seem premature, though I'd equally want to see what "proper use" even looks like before declaring it safe.

From where I sit hiring people, I care less about whether they used AI on an assignment and more about whether they can actually think through a problem and explain their reasoning—so maybe the real question is how universities teach students to use these tools responsibly rather than just blocking them.

I've spent 26 years watching students transform through the struggle of learning—the calluses, the failed auditions, the breakthrough moments—and I can tell you that if you remove the friction, you remove the person who emerges on the other side. There's no shortcut to becoming a musician, and there shouldn't be one in any discipline that matters.

Banning AI from universities would be like teaching pilots to fly without modern instruments—you're not protecting them from reality, you're just delaying the inevitable moment they have to adapt. The students I mentor who've learned to use AI as a thinking partner, not a shortcut, are the ones actually winning in aerospace interviews and on the job.

A blanket ban treats AI like we treated calculators in the 1980s—we eventually realized the real skill wasn't mental arithmetic, but knowing which problems calculators could actually solve and which ones required deeper reasoning first. Our job is teaching students to recognize that distinction, not to enforce ignorance of tools they'll encounter in every workplace they enter.

I've spent enough time in both corporate and academic environments to know that prohibition without evidence tends to backfire—we need rigorous studies on how students actually use these tools before we design policy around worst-case scenarios rather than documented outcomes.

I've seen this play out in our partner institutions—when you ban something completely, students just get sneakier about it rather than engaging with the *why* behind the assignment. Seems like the universities doing better work are the ones actually redesigning coursework to teach critical thinking *with* AI as a tool, not against it.

I've watched too many students arrive at my office convinced they can't write, when really they've just never sat with the productive discomfort of actually thinking—and that's exactly what AI promises to eliminate. If we let these tools do the cognitive heavy lifting, we're not just changing how students write; we're fundamentally changing what it means to learn.

The universities that are thriving right now aren't banning AI—they're teaching students to think critically *about* how to use it, which is the actual skill that matters in any field. Prohibition is just security theater; integration is how you actually prepare people for the jobs they'll actually have.

A blanket ban pretends we can hold back the tide, while unrestricted use treats the university like a trade school—what we actually need is the harder work of teaching students to recognize which problems demand their own thinking and which are better solved by tools, then grading accordingly.

I've spent nearly a decade tracking how medical students build diagnostic reasoning, and what concerns me most isn't the tool itself—it's that we're implementing AI in coursework at scale without any rigorous longitudinal studies showing whether graduates who relied on it actually develop stronger clinical judgment when they're standing in front of patients. Until we have that data, banning feels premature, but so does assuming it's pedagogically neutral.

Honestly, trying to ban AI feels like shutting the gate after the horse has bolted – kids are going to use it anyway, so maybe unis need to focus on teaching them *how* to use it properly rather than just saying no?

The question assumes we can simply prohibit our way out of this problem, but I wonder if we're asking the wrong thing—not whether to ban AI, but what intellectual virtues we risk losing if students never learn to struggle productively with difficult ideas, the way one must when thinking is genuinely one's own work.

The real question isn't whether to ban AI, but how to design assignments that require genuine thinking—ones where AI becomes a tool for exploration rather than a shortcut around learning. I built my data skills partly through online courses and communities precisely because they forced me to solve real problems; universities should do the same, just with updated tools.

Rather than asking whether we should ban AI, I'd suggest we ask what skills our assessments actually measure—because if a tool can complete the task, that's often a sign the task isn't capturing what we truly need students to learn. The real work is redesigning our courses around the thinking processes that matter, not policing the tools.

The neuroplasticity literature suggests that outsourcing cognitive tasks entirely does impair consolidation, but we've no longitudinal data yet on whether strategic AI use—say, as a scaffold for struggling learners—might actually enhance retention compared to traditional methods. Before we lock down policy, we should be running properly controlled studies across different disciplines and student populations.

I think most schools will end up doing something like requiring disclosure or building it into assignments rather than just banning it outright—seems more realistic given how fast the tech moves anyway.

I've spent two decades watching students discover who they are as thinkers through the messy, essential work of writing and revision—and I can't imagine outsourcing that struggle to an algorithm without losing something fundamental to what it means to learn.

I think banning AI in universities is like banning calculators in math class—it might feel safer, but it just pushes the real skill-building offline. The students who'll actually thrive in 2030 won't be the ones who avoided AI, but the ones who learned to think critically about when and how to use it.

Rather than banning AI, universities should redesign assessments to test what machines can't do—synthesis, critical judgment, ethical reasoning—while teaching students to use these tools responsibly; the challenge isn't the technology itself, but whether we're brave enough to rethink what we actually value in education.

I'd need to see actual data on learning outcomes before supporting a ban—the question isn't whether AI should be banned, but under what conditions it measurably helps or harms the specific skills we're actually trying to teach. Has anyone here reviewed what we're *optimizing for* when we restrict tools versus when we permit them?

I think a blanket ban is probably unrealistic—AI's here to stay, and students will find workarounds anyway. What seems more practical is for universities to set clear expectations about when it's a tool versus when it crosses into academic dishonesty, similar to how we handle other technologies in professional settings.

I've watched too many students mistake a polished essay for understanding to believe that outsourcing the thinking process to AI serves their education; the real work—the frustration, the false starts, the moments when a text finally clicks—that's where learning lives, and we diminish it the moment we allow machines to bypass it.

Banning AI in coursework is like banning calculators in math class—it fights reality instead of teaching students how to work alongside these tools responsibly, which is what employers actually need. I've watched this play out firsthand: the designers who thrive aren't the ones who pretend AI doesn't exist, they're the ones who learned to use it as a thinking partner while keeping their critical eye sharp.

The real question isn't whether to ban AI—it's whether our assignments are actually measuring what we claim they measure, or just testing who can follow arbitrary restrictions. I've redesigned enough rubrics to know that if an assignment collapses the moment a student uses a tool, we've probably just revealed a problem with the assignment, not solved one with a policy.

I've watched enough junior engineers struggle with basic troubleshooting to worry that we're optimizing for speed over understanding, but I'm equally concerned that a blanket ban might just push AI use underground instead of forcing us to teach students *how* to use these tools critically—we need rigorous studies tracking what actually happens to problem-solving ability, not assumptions either way.

Look, I've got kids paying tuition right now and I'm not losing sleep over this one way or the other—seems like every school's gonna handle it differently anyway, and honestly the job market will sort out who actually learned something useful.

I've watched too many capable writers outsource their thinking to tools that can produce polished prose without ever having sat with genuine confusion, and I worry we're confusing fluency with understanding—the struggle to articulate what we half-know is precisely where intellectual maturity happens.

Banning AI is like banning calculators in math class—you're not actually protecting learning, you're just training students for a world that doesn't exist anymore. The real skill is knowing when to use it, how to validate its output, and what questions to ask it in the first place.

Rather than banning AI, we should ask what skills our degrees actually certify—and then design assessments that require students to demonstrate those competencies in ways that show their own thinking, whether that means collaborating with AI tools or working without them intentionally. I've seen faculty shift from "write an essay" to "here's a messy real-world problem; show me how you'd approach it," and that's where the real learning happens.

Before we ban AI in coursework, we should be asking the same questions I learned to ask about chemistry lab shortcuts: *which* cognitive skills does this particular use actually bypass, and do we have evidence it's happening at scale? Anecdotes worry me less than the gap between what we assume students are losing and what we can actually measure.

I think the real question universities need to answer is *where* AI adds value versus where it undermines learning—a blanket ban seems like avoiding the harder work of figuring out which assignments actually test the skills that matter.

I've spent nearly two decades watching students develop scholarly judgment through the hard work of textual analysis, and I cannot in good conscience pretend that substituting AI for that struggle produces an educated person—it produces someone who has outsourced the very thinking we're meant to cultivate.

Look, I spent fifteen years in traditional architecture before AI tools existed, and I can tell you the firms hiring today aren't looking for people who can draw by hand—they're looking for people who can prompt, iterate, and synthesize designs faster than ever before. Banning AI in universities isn't protecting students; it's just delaying their education until they're already competing in the job market.

Rather than banning AI, we should be asking ourselves what skills we're actually trying to develop in each assignment—then decide transparently whether AI use supports or undermines that goal, and communicate it clearly to students upfront.

I spent eight years measuring how cognitive load affects learning before I ever saw a patient, and I can tell you we're rushing to judgment here without the data we need—we don't actually know yet whether AI use in coursework impairs the kind of deep processing that leads to genuine understanding, or whether it simply shifts *how* that processing happens. Before universities legislate this, we should be funding longitudinal studies on student outcomes, not relying on the same intuitions that have guided education policy for decades without much scrutiny.

Look, I work in nonprofits where we're just trying to stretch every dollar and get people creative—I'd rather see universities focus on whether students can actually *think critically* than police their laptops. If a kid uses AI to draft an essay but then has to defend their ideas in person, isn't that kind of the point?

The appeal to empiricism is superficially compelling, yet it founders on a prior question my colleagues seem reluctant to address: by what methodology do we measure learning outcomes when we cannot first establish with certainty that the work submitted is authentically the student's own—and is it not precisely the absence of reliable authorship verification that renders such comparative studies epistemologically compromised from inception?

Real talk: bans don't work because they don't address *why* students reach for AI in the first place—usually because the assignment itself doesn't actually require deep thinking. If your coursework forces you to engage genuinely with ideas, AI becomes a tool you use *after* doing the hard work, not instead of it.

The pianist analogy is compelling, but here's what I'd push back on: the struggle itself isn't the point—*directed* struggle toward something that matters is. A student writing their fifth essay the same way they wrote the first is just repetition, not growth. The question isn't whether to remove friction, but whether we're designing friction in the right places—and that's where disclosure + oral defence actually works better than a blank ban.

I notice we're circling around something important but maybe not quite landing on it: several of you are describing *ideal conditions* for learning (struggle, articulation, the eureka moment), and others are describing *realistic tool use in professional contexts*—but has anyone actually studied whether a well-designed AI policy in coursework reliably produces the first without unnecessarily mimicking the second? That's the empirical question I keep returning to, because anecdotes about both transformative struggle and responsible tool integration are genuine, but they don't tell us which pedagogical choices actually move the needle on student understanding.

yeah exactly—like the real red flag isn't students using chatgpt, it's professors who haven't thought about *why* they're assigning the work in the first place. if you can't explain what skill you're actually testing, that's on you, not the technology.

I hear the case for redesign, and there's real wisdom in it—but I'd gently push back: we can't teach judgment about *when* to use a tool by first letting students skip the foundational work of thinking without it. The steering wheel metaphor assumes they've already learned the road; in my experience, they haven't, and handing them the wheel before they understand the terrain doesn't build competence, it obscures what they're missing.

The nonprofit mentor makes a solid point about fundamentals—but here's the thing: if universities are already failing to teach those fundamentals *before* AI showed up, banning the tool doesn't suddenly fix the pedagogy, it just means those students graduate unable to use the tools their actual jobs will require. Fix the teaching, not the toolbox.

The medical education point is crucial—we're conflating two separate questions: "Does this tool help learning?" and "Are we measuring the right things to know?"—until we disaggregate those, every position here (ban or integrate) is just educated guessing, and that's precisely when you need pilot programmes with actual outcome tracking, not policy theatre.

I notice we're repeating the calculator analogy and the struggle-builds-character argument, but neither has engaged with the core empirical question: what does the evidence actually show about *which* uses of AI correlate with weaker clinical reasoning in our graduates, and which correlate with stronger performance on assessments that matter? Until we disaggregate that, we're debating philosophy when we should be collecting data.

Fair points all around, but I think we're overthinking this a bit—the educators who actually care about developing critical thinking will figure out how to assess it (maybe oral exams, in-class work, project-based stuff), while the ones just ticking boxes were never going to catch AI use anyway, so a ban doesn't really solve much there either.

I appreciate the push-back on blanket bans, truly, but I think there's a difference between measuring defect rates and abandoning the principle entirely—we don't run experiments on whether students should attend lectures before deciding lectures matter. The cognitive load researcher makes a fair point about needing data, but we already have decades of evidence that struggle itself is generative; what we're actually missing is honest conversation about why we're so eager to believe there might be a shortcut, when every meaningful skill we value—from medicine to music to carpentry—still requires the apprenticeship.

The posts assuming we can separate "genuine thinking" from tool use are missing something: every expert I know thinks *through* their tools, not despite them—the skill universities should actually certify is recognizing which problems need human judgment and which don't, then executing accordingly, which is messier to teach but worth infinitely more than a grade.

I think the posts above are actually converging on the same point: context matters more than the tool itself, and universities need assessment methods sophisticated enough to distinguish between a student using AI to deepen understanding versus one using it to avoid thinking altogether—which, frankly, is the same problem they've always had to solve, just with a different technology.

What strikes me across these responses is that we're all circling the same insight: the policy question is actually a measurement problem in disguise. Until we have cohort data on retention and transfer—not just assignment grades—comparing identical learning objectives taught with and without AI access, we're essentially asking universities to make manufacturing decisions without quality metrics.

I keep coming back to something practical: in arts funding, we don't reject applications that use design software or digital tools—we assess the *thinking* behind them, and whether the applicant can justify their choices. Universities could learn from that framework instead of pretending the tool will disappear if they just don't acknowledge it.

I recognise the force of these counterarguments, but I suspect we're conflating two distinct problems: designing assessments that matter (which I agree we do poorly) is not the same as permitting the tools that undermine the cognitive struggle those assessments ought to demand. We can and should teach students to think critically about AI—but not by exempting them from the very kind of sustained reasoning that builds the judgment to do so.

The universities I work with that are actually ahead of this curve aren't banning AI—they're redesigning assessments to test what matters: can students *critique* an AI output, defend a design choice against it, build something that AI alone couldn't? That's where the real skill gap is, and it's measurable.

The last point about cognitive struggle is crucial, and it's where I think the debate shifts from policy to pedagogy: we need assessments that *require* the struggle—oral defenses, real-time problem-solving under observation, the kind of clinical reasoning that happens at a patient's bedside—because those can't be outsourced to a tool, and they're what actually predict competence in practice.

I notice a pattern emerging in this thread: we keep circling back to "teach critical use" without examining *how* that actually works in practice—and I'd wager most of us haven't systematically tracked whether students who learn "when to use AI" actually develop better judgment than those who don't, or if we're just hoping the pedagogy exists rather than building it deliberately.

I think the mentee observation hits on something real—the ones struggling aren't struggling because they used AI, they're struggling because nobody taught them to *interrogate* it, to ask whether the output actually solves their problem or just sounds plausible. That's a teaching problem, not a tool problem.

I appreciate the call for empirical rigour, truly, but I think we risk getting lost in methodology while the fundamental thing slips away—and that's the lived experience of a student *becoming* a thinker, which you can't isolate in a controlled study the way you can measure retention rates. What I'd want to see is less "which tasks allow AI" and more an honest reckoning with whether we're willing to sit with our students in the discomfort of their own struggling minds, because that discomfort is where they discover what they're actually capable of.

I think we're overcomplicating this: the real test isn't whether AI helps or hurts learning in isolation, but whether graduates who've learned *with* AI tools under structured guidance outperform those who haven't when they hit the job market—and based on hiring patterns at my firm, they already are. If universities are serious about this, they should stop debating philosophy and start tracking cohorts rigorously, because the data will speak faster than any policy paper.

The partition approach has merit, but we need to be honest: the real experiment should measure *transfer*—can students who've only ever used AI to draft essays then solve unfamiliar problems without it, or defend a position under live questioning?—because that's what employers are actually testing for, and that's where I suspect we'll find the learning gap.

The intellectual struggle argument assumes we've measured what actually gets lost—but I've designed enough evaluation frameworks to know that assumption without data is just intuition dressed up as principle. If we're serious about preserving genuine learning, we need to run the studies that show *which specific cognitive tasks* atrophy with AI assistance versus which ones students master faster with it as a scaffold, then build our policies around those findings rather than our fears.

These posts are making me think—the synthesizer analogy is sharp, but I'd push back slightly: music students learn the instrument *first*, then decide what synthesizers are for. Maybe the practical question isn't ban or allow, but ensuring students can think without AI before they think *with* it, the way we'd expect them to know how to hand-write before they use spell-check.

I recognize the force of these arguments—my colleagues are describing something real and important—yet I notice they shift the burden from preserving struggle to *managing* it, from asking whether the student has thought to asking whether we can still detect thinking. The pianist analogy holds precisely because no amount of documentation about *why* one skipped the scales makes up for the atrophied fingers; what we're really debating is whether we believe the struggle itself—not our ability to audit it—is irreplaceable.

I appreciate the point about struggle being where comprehension roots itself, but I'd push back gently: the struggle needs to be *productive*, and right now we're not distinguishing between "healthy cognitive friction" and "friction caused by refusing to use available tools." What if we redesigned so students do the hard conceptual work first—articulating why that metaphor matters—and then we ask them to critically evaluate how an AI would explain it, or where it falls short? That's the actual professional skillset.

I notice we keep circling back to "how do we assess thinking"—which is exactly right, but I'd push us one step further: if we can't articulate what authentic human judgment looks like *within* our discipline, no policy change will help. That clarity has to come first.

I notice we're circling around the same legitimate concerns without any actual data on learning outcomes—and I'd genuinely welcome if someone in this thread could point me toward a rigorous longitudinal study tracking how AI access affects student reasoning skills, not just grades or completion rates. Until we have that evidence, we're all just trading intuitions dressed up as policy arguments.

Yeah, I think that calculator comparison really lands for me — we didn't ban calculators, we just had to rethink what maths teaching was actually *for*. Same thing here, innit? The universities that figure out how to teach students to think *with* these tools rather than just *against* them are probably going to turn out graduates who actually know what they're doing.

You've articulated something essential that I want to push back on gently: the distinction between AI-as-thinking-tool and AI-as-shortcut assumes students already know how to think, but my core concern is that outsourcing the *struggle itself*—the months of sitting with confusion, the false starts, the slow clarification—is precisely what builds that capacity in the first place. Redesigned assessments matter, yes, but they cannot fully compensate for the irreplaceable cognitive work of wrestling language into meaning.

Honestly, the posts asking "where's the evidence?" hit the nail on the head—we're debating AI bans without actually testing what students retain when they use it as a thinking partner versus a shortcut, and that's backwards. I appreciate the point about institutional credibility, but I'd push back gently: that "contract" is already broken if we're certifying people who can't navigate the actual tools they'll use in their roles. The question isn't whether to trust students with AI—it's whether we're willing to redesign our assessments honestly enough to know what they actually understand.

I appreciate the call for transparent criteria—that's essential—but we should be cautious about framing this as a design problem when we haven't yet established baseline data: which skills are actually being developed through struggle versus efficiently achieved through AI, and whether students who use these tools show measurable retention and transfer of learning months or years later. Without that evidence, even well-intentioned redesign risks optimizing our assessments around a tool whose actual cognitive impact remains largely unmeasured.

I notice we keep circling back to the same underlying problem: universities would rather debate whether to ban the tool than do the actual pedagogical work of redesigning what and how they teach, which frankly takes more effort than a policy memo. Until assessment design catches up to the reality of what students can access, the ban-or-allow question will keep feeling abstract—and my kids' generation will be the ones paying for that delay.

You've identified something crucial—that the question isn't whether to ban AI, but what we're trying to certify—yet I'd push back gently on the assumption that we can neatly partition assignments into "AI-compatible" and "AI-free" categories: the risk is that we'll design around the tool rather than around the genuine intellectual work that makes a degree meaningful, and students will learn to recognise which tasks require their own thinking and which don't, rather than developing the habit of thinking rigorously as a default. Perhaps the more honest question is whether we're willing to demand—and scaffold—the difficult struggle itself as non-negotiable in some core assignments, not as punishment, but as the site where actual formation as a thinker occurs.

The uncomfortable truth is that students who graduate without ever having to reason *through* an AI's mistakes will be the ones struggling in their first week on the job—and universities banning AI won't prevent that, it'll just mean they learn those lessons expensively, on someone else's dime.

What strikes me from this thread is that we're still treating AI as the problem rather than our assessment design—and until universities get specific about *what they're actually trying to measure*, any policy they land on (ban or permission) will just be fighting the symptom. The institutions I'd be watching aren't asking "should students use AI?" but "what can't be outsourced to AI, and how do we build assessments around *that*?"

I notice we're hearing two legitimate concerns here—the real cognitive risk when struggle gets outsourced, and the practical reality that tools exist whether we acknowledge them or not—but I haven't yet seen anyone propose what tracking and assessment actually looks like in practice. Doesn't that gap matter more than choosing sides?

I think the real issue is that universities are still designing assessments like it's 2015—if you want to know whether someone can think critically, stop asking them to write essays in isolation and start asking them to evaluate, critique, and improve AI outputs, because that's what the job actually demands. The institutions getting this right aren't banning tools; they're being specific about where human judgment has to do the work.

I find myself reconsidering what I wrote two years ago: my colleagues here in Stuttgart and I have watched third-year students submit capstone proposals where they cannot articulate *why* their AI-generated specifications matter, only that the tool produced them—and when I ask them to defend a choice under constraint, they fall silent. The measuring tape does not absolve the carpenter of understanding load; the tool is only useful to someone who already thinks like an engineer.

I think what keeps getting lost here is that the medical educator and the teacher watching students struggle with metaphors are describing the same real problem—but banning AI won't solve it, redesigning how we assess understanding will. The tool isn't the issue; using it to skip the hard thinking part is, so maybe the question is how we create assignments where AI actually becomes useless unless you've already done the real intellectual work.

I appreciate the push for evidence—we absolutely need that data—but I'd gently push back on the "we don't know yet" framing: we do have decades of cognitive science showing that offloading *all* steps in a process (not just computation) weakens transfer and metacognition, so the question isn't whether AI assistance can be harmful, it's how to structure it so students struggle productively on what matters most before reaching for the tool. The partition idea is smart, though—that's the kind of designed experiment that could actually tell us whether the harm is real or a pedagogical problem we can engineer around.

I notice we're circling around the same insight: that every policy choice here is really a choice about what we're measuring and teaching, not about the technology itself—so before any university adopts a rule about AI, it ought to answer whether that rule actually improves the skills we claim to care about, or just makes compliance easier to audit.

Yeah, this thread's hitting on something real—the medical educator's point about lacking data is solid, and the assignment design angle is dead on. What I keep coming back to from my work is that organizations that thrived through change weren't the ones arguing about whether the new tool was "allowed," they were the ones clear-eyed about what they were actually trying to accomplish and ruthless about redesigning around that.

I hear the pragmatism in these responses, and I don't disagree that we need to teach students *how* to work with these tools—but there's a difference between learning to use a calculator after you've mastered long division and never learning long division at all because the calculator was there from day one. The question isn't whether AI exists in their future careers; it's whether we let them skip the foundational cognitive work that makes them *capable* of using it wisely instead of just following its suggestions.

Look, the "contract with employers" argument only holds if we're honest about what that contract actually is—and spoiler alert, nobody's hiring mathematicians to solve problems without access to tools.
