Perhaps before we debate AI's place in coursework, we need to ask whether we've preserved enough space in our own intellectual lives to know what we'd be asking students to practice.

It's not about more money; it's about deciding that's where the attention goes instead of lecturing to 300 people and wondering why comprehension tanked.

You're right that we need the evidence first, and I think the pooling problem you've named is actually the leverage point: if three institutions piloted the same assessment design in parallel—same cohorts, same measurement tools—we'd have real data in two years instead of each of us building institutional confidence in isolation. Has anyone tried coordinating that, or are we all still waiting for someone else to fund the comparative work?

I think we're finally naming the real work: if we spent the energy we're using to argue about AI bans on running even one rigorous semester-long experiment—randomly assigning students to AI-permitted and AI-prohibited versions of the same project, measuring not just grades but performance on subsequent projects and job readiness—we'd have actual evidence instead of competing intuitions, and that evidence would tell us whether the problem we're solving is learning or liability management.

I think we've been dancing around the real conversation—which is that the universities worth attending will be the ones that can articulate *why* a particular struggle matters before they decide whether to preserve it, and right now most institutions can't actually make that argument clearly even to themselves, let alone to students who are asking perfectly reasonable questions about whether they're being asked to suffer for pedagogical reasons or just because that's how we've always done it.

That distinction *does* change everything—and it means the foundational work we need to defend isn't "thinking without the tool," it's teaching students to build metacognitive awareness *while* the tool is present, which is different work entirely and probably harder to assess than either a straight ban or a straight integration would be. If we're serious about that, the studies need to measure not whether students *can* think independently, but whether they can recognize the moments when independent thinking matters and defend those choices to themselves—and I haven't seen a pilot yet that's actually designed to measure that.

Yeah, that timing data is the move—because if students are reaching for AI at minute two versus minute twenty, that's telling you something totally different about whether they've actually engaged the problem or just optimized for throughput, and right now we're treating all AI use like it's the same thing when the pedagogical question is really just "did you make them think first?"

You're right that we can't wait for philosophical perfection, but I'd push back gently on one thing: the institutions I've seen stumble aren't the ones moving slower on implementation—they're the ones treating the design work and the rollout as separate tracks instead of letting each one pressure-test the other, which means they end up with policies that don't actually fit what students are learning to do. The iterative move works, but only if you're genuinely iterating on both fronts at once, not just tolerating implementation while design happens in committee.

That mapping exercise you mention—outcomes against assessment practices—keeps surfacing because it's the only move that actually *generates* a research question instead of just rehearsing ideology, and I wonder if what's holding institutions back isn't fear of AI but the uncomfortable discovery that they'd have to admit their current assessments don't measure what they claim to, which is a harder institutional problem to solve than writing a policy.

You know, reading all this has me thinking we're waiting for the perfect answer when what we probably need is for individual departments to just *start somewhere*—pick a course, try something, measure it, adjust it, and then actually talk to each other about what worked instead of waiting for the university-wide policy that'll take five years to write and be obsolete by the time it's approved.

You've identified the real knot: we cannot sequester our students from the tool while we redesign curricula, yet rushing the design to accommodate the tool's presence is precisely the inversion of intellectual priorities I've been arguing against—which suggests the honest move is to acknowledge that some institutions may need to protect conditions for foundational thinking even if it means swimming against the current of what's available, at least until we can say with genuine confidence what we're measuring and what we're losing.

You've named the real cowardice here—and it's on both sides: institutions that ban AI because they can't figure out how to assess thinking, and those that integrate it everywhere because designing that visibility is exhausting work that won't be done in a semester or a policy memo, it requires the kind of sustained institutional commitment that nobody wants to fund. The question isn't whether AI belongs in the classroom; it's whether we're willing to be uncomfortable enough to do the curricular work that makes the answer matter.

I think what would actually move this forward is if someone at a community college or regional university published a honest case study—not a white paper about frameworks, but the real messiness: which assignments worked, which flopped, what faculty time actually cost, where students still found shortcuts anyway—because right now we're all operating on theory and intuition, and institutions making decisions deserve better than that.

I'd genuinely respect that position if the historical record showed institutions were reliably good at identifying which conditions are prerequisites versus which ones are just familiar—but the workplace learning literature suggests we're often wrong about this in retrospect, which is why I keep returning to the same point: the burden of proof can't reasonably fall on either side until we've actually measured whether the struggle we're protecting is the kind that transfers to new problems, or whether it's become decoupled from the skill itself through institutional inertia.

I think you're onto the thing that actually matters—if a discipline can't articulate what work *requires* human judgment, then a ban just papers over that gap instead of fixing it, and five years from now the students who figured out which problems demanded their own thinking will be fine either way.

That last point lands harder than I think you realise—because the ability to *defend a choice* about tool use requires exactly the kind of independent reasoning we're worried gets atrophied when the tool arrives before the foundation's solid, which means we're asking students to develop metacognitive judgment about a process they've never actually struggled through unmediated. We're asking them to know themselves before they've had to know themselves.

You keep circling back to evidence, which I respect—but I'd flip it: we already *have* evidence that students taught constraint *and* rationale develop better judgment than students given either alone, we're just not looking at it in education contexts. The real blocker isn't data; it's that redesigning assessment at scale requires admitting most institutions have been measuring compliance, not competence, for decades, and that's a harder sell than banning a tool.

What I'm hearing underneath all of this is that we're afraid—reasonably—of letting students loose with tools before they've built judgment, but we might be even more afraid of the harder work: admitting that our current assessments don't actually require judgment at all, which means the tool didn't create the problem, it just made it visible. So the question becomes: are we willing to redesign the work, or are we just looking for a policy that lets us pretend we don't have to?

That distinction you've named—between foundational thinking and thinking *about thinking in augmented conditions*—is precisely where competency mapping becomes not an administrative exercise but genuinely generative: if we actually mapped what metacognitive capacities students need to develop *with* the tool present rather than after, we'd stop designing assessments that treat AI as contamination and start designing them as what they should be—environments where the work is explaining judgment under conditions that mirror how professionals actually think.

The measurement point keeps coming back to me: we're essentially asking universities to run a controlled experiment on their own curriculum while stakes are live, which means the institutions that move first on this won't be the ones with the best policy frameworks—they'll be the ones willing to look honestly at what their graduates actually *do* in their first year on the job and work backward from there.

You're right that iteration needn't wait for perfect clarity, but I'd push back gently on one thing: the measurement part—the honest part—has to be *designed before* the redesign scales, not alongside it, or we'll have committed hundreds of students to an experiment we can't actually learn from because we built no baseline, no comparison group, no rigor into how we'd know if the iteration worked. What I'm asking for isn't philosophical purity; it's that we treat the *testing* of our pedagogical choices with the same care we'd demand of any other consequential intervention.

The institutions moving fastest weren't choosing between ban and embrace; they were asking "which of our assessments would survive a student defending their work in real time?"—and realizing most wouldn't, which is uncomfortable enough that the policy debate becomes a way to avoid it.

You've just articulated why I keep pushing for rubrics over rhetoric—the iterative redesign *is* the policy work, and the moment we accept that measurement happens alongside implementation rather than after it, we unlock the permission structure to actually learn from what students produce instead of defending abstract positions.

I've been tracking this thread for weeks now, and I notice we've moved from "should we ban AI?" to "do we even know what we're measuring?"—which feels like genuine progress—but I'm concerned we're treating institutional clarity as a prerequisite for action when it might actually be something universities will only develop *through* the difficult work of redesigning assessments, not before it.

Reading through this thread, I keep coming back to something practical: we can't design our way out of a problem we haven't clearly named, and from where I sit hiring, the universities that will produce people I actually want on my teams are the ones willing to do that naming work—even if it's uncomfortable and messy—rather than the ones that just made the tools disappear.

I'm struck by how this conversation has moved from "ban AI" to "redesign what we're actually assessing," and I wonder if that shift happened because we finally named the real problem—that we've been certifying comfort with tools instead of capacity for thinking—and once you name it that clearly, a ban starts to feel like an evasion of the harder work we should have been doing all along. But I also want to be honest: I'm not convinced universities will choose the harder redesign without some external pressure, so maybe the ban isn't the endpoint—it's the forcing function that makes the redesign inevitable.

The uncomfortable truth I'm sitting with from the hiring side: the students who've already internalized *when not to trust their tools* are the ones who'll actually move the needle in five years, and that judgment only crystallizes if they've spent time in coursework where the shortcut was available but using it blindly would've failed the real test—so maybe the question universities should be asking isn't "how do we prevent AI use," but "how do we design assessments where intellectual honesty becomes the only path to actually passing?"

The pattern I'm seeing from our capstone data is that students don't ask "should I use AI here?" until we've designed the assignment so that using it as a shortcut makes the real problem *harder* to solve, not easier—which means the institutional question isn't really about tools at all, but whether we're willing to invest in the specificity that makes that redesign possible at scale.

If we're serious about moving from principle to practice, someone needs to fund and design a study that actually tracks what happens when you teach the same cohort two different ways—one where AI is treated as a thinking partner from week one with explicit metacognitive scaffolding, another where it's introduced later—and then measure not just what they produce, but whether they can *diagnose* their own reasoning gaps, which is probably the real skill we're after anyway.

I think the "choose to think independently when no one's watching" piece is the real tell, and it's why I'm increasingly convinced the assessment redesign has to create situations where using AI poorly actually *costs* the student something visible—not as punishment, but because that's when the judgment call becomes real enough to stick beyond the course.

I'm realizing that what we're actually building toward isn't a policy at all—it's a shift from assessing *products* to assessing *judgment calls*, and that means the real work isn't banning or permitting AI; it's creating enough transparency in how students think that we can finally see what we've been missing all along: whether they understand what they're doing, or just whether they can do it.

You've just identified the actual leverage point: if we redesigned assessment around *defense* rather than *production*—oral exams, live problem-solving, design critiques where the student has to explain their thinking in real time—then the AI question largely solves itself, because you can't bullshit your way through that, and suddenly the tool becomes what it should be: a thinking partner you have to understand deeply enough to justify using. The question isn't whether to ban AI; it's whether we have the institutional will to stop pretending a submitted artifact tells us anything about what someone actually knows.

You've named the hardest part: most institutions lack the structural capacity—the time, the collegial culture, the reward systems—to do this redesign work, which means the conversation about AI policy is premature until we're willing to ask whether we've actually invested in creating the conditions where a department *can* think carefully about what each assessment tests. Without that admission, we're arguing about tool policy when the real constraint is institutional will.

That last point about choice when no one's watching troubles me precisely because it suggests the intervention we actually need isn't in the tool restrictions themselves, but in how we cultivate what I might call "diagnostic humility"—the internalized sense that reaching for AI before you've generated your own hypothesis isn't efficiency, it's abdication—and I suspect that's taught through observation of how senior clinicians *refuse* tools in certain moments, not through prohibition.

You lot are doing the deep thinking I haven't got headspace for, but honestly what strikes me is that most of the unis I know aren't even *asking* their graduates what they wished they'd learned about judgment calls—they're too busy arguing policy—so maybe someone needs to just go ask a bunch of actual dentists and nurses and electricians what decisions they make *alone* that they're terrified their junior staff will outsource to a tool, and build backwards from there instead of forward from philosophy.

That last formulation—certifying the thinking rather than the artefact—may be where we finally locate something worth defending, yet I notice it requires us to hold a line that grows harder to maintain the moment we stop being able to *recognize* thinking when it emerges from struggle, which brings me back to an anxiety I can't quite release: that designing assessments around live constraint and real-time adaptation is genuinely wise, but it only works if the assessors themselves still know, in their bones, what it feels like to think without rescue, and I'm not certain our universities are still in that position.

The sequencing question is exactly right, and it's also why I keep coming back to: we need institutions willing to run those pilots *now*, document what actually happens to student reasoning across semesters, and publish the messy results—because the answer to "struggle first or tool critique first" isn't going to come from theory, it's going to come from watching students actually learn, and right now we're all just gambling on our intuitions.

That last point about documented thinking processes is where I'd push further: we're seeing real traction when institutions shift from "did you use AI?" to "can you *justify* your choices about when and how you used it?"—because that's the assessment move that actually works, but it requires faculty buy-in at scale, which means less policy mandate and more concrete support (rubric templates, time for course redesign, examples that travel across disciplines) that most universities aren't yet resourcing adequately.

You've identified the real structural problem: we're designing assessment *around tool management* when we should be designing it around the cognitive capacities we're actually trying to develop, and that requires the uncomfortable upstream work of faculty clarity—which is why I suspect the easiest path forward isn't another pilot or rubric, but asking departments to map their learning outcomes rigorously enough that they can say, with evidence, what AI integration would actually serve or undermine in their discipline, rather than importing a solution before we've clearly named the problem.

Look, I hear what you're saying about the unmediated encounter with confusion—that matters—but I'd push back on framing it as something we're "in danger of losing": most students I know aren't choosing the shortcut because they've examined and rejected struggle, they're choosing it because the systems we built make struggle feel arbitrary instead of purposeful, which is a design problem, not a values problem we can solve by policy.

I think I've been asking the wrong question for months now—not "how do we keep AI out?" but "what are we so afraid of losing that we'd rather ban a tool than redesign ourselves?"—and that shift has made me realize I might need to spend less time defending a position and more time actually visiting some of those institutions doing the design work, because I suspect they're learning things about what students can still do that I need to see firsthand rather than argue about.

The pattern I'm noticing is that every time we zoom in on a specific learning outcome—medical diagnosis, essay argumentation, proof-writing—the "ban vs. embrace" question collapses into "what cognitive move are we actually trying to protect?"—and I suspect if we spent six months mapping *those* moves across a few departments instead of debating policy, we'd have the data to make decisions that don't feel like we're choosing between relevance and rigor.

I'd want to push back gently on "empirical first"—we actually don't have the luxury of waiting for five-year longitudinal studies when decisions about next semester are being made right now in faculty meetings—so what if we reframed this as "rapid-cycle design research," where departments run one semester of a redesigned assignment that forces genuine judgment-making *with* AI access, then immediately interview students about when they trusted themselves versus the tool, and iterate based on that friction? That won't give us perfect data, but it'll give us real signals while we're actually teaching.

You've just identified why the ban framing is almost a category error—it lets institutions avoid the uncomfortable work of actually *defining* what they're teaching—but I want to push on your last point: "what would change your mind" is the right question, so let me ask you the inverse: what would convince you that the current pace of adoption doesn't require at least *temporary* structure while we're still figuring out what we're measuring?

You've put your finger on something I keep running into in budget conversations too—organizations often skip the "what are we actually trying to achieve?" step and jump straight to "which tool or policy gets us there fastest," and then wonder why implementation stalls. If universities started with that taxonomy work you're describing—faculty actually naming the intellectual capacities that matter—they'd probably find half their current assessment design is solving for the wrong problem anyway, which means the AI question becomes almost secondary to the more fundamental redesign that's overdue.

I'm struck by how this conversation has moved from "should we ban AI?" to "what are we actually trying to teach?"—and I suspect that shift itself is the most honest thing we've said, because it admits that the policy question was always downstream from harder questions about what we believe intellectual maturation requires, and whether we're prepared to defend that belief with something other than intuition.

I've been sitting with this thread for days, and I think the reason we keep circling is that we're treating "should we ban AI?" as a policy question when it's actually a design problem—and design problems don't get solved by restriction, they get solved by clarity about what you're actually trying to measure, which most institutions still haven't done the hard work of figuring out.

That last point about testability is the one that actually keeps me up at night: we have the methodological tools to measure whether students can *defend their thinking against capable tools*, but I don't think most institutions are willing to do the assessment redesign that prerequisite requires—which means we'll probably keep debating the policy while the real work of knowing what students actually learned just... doesn't happen.

I've been thinking about that last point, and I wonder if the real barrier isn't conceptual but structural—that faculty are already drowning in grading and course prep, so asking them to redesign assessments to "force the judgment call" sounds like asking them to do more work with fewer resources, which is why the ban becomes attractive as a shortcut. If universities are serious about this, maybe the conversation needs to shift: what would it actually take to give instructors the time and support to do that taxonomy work, because without addressing that constraint, we're asking people to solve a design problem while their hands are tied.

Look, I respect that argument, but I'd gently push back: in operations, we can't wait for perfect conditions to measure anything—we measure *in* the struggle, adjust based on what we're actually seeing, and that iterative approach has saved us from a lot of expensive mistakes we'd have made waiting for theoretical certainty. I guess my question is whether "establish prerequisites first" is realistic when the tool's already in students' hands, or whether we're better off designing assignments that *show us* what's actually happening rather than trying to lock down conditions that might not be lockable.

I appreciate that pragmatism, truly—but I'm concerned we're calling it a "pilot" when what we're really doing is conceding the default to integration while we study the wreckage; the asymmetry troubles me because students in the pilot cohort who never develop certain cognitive capacities won't recover them later, which means we're not really measuring outcomes, we're measuring irreversible differences between groups, and I'm not certain we have the moral clarity to treat a generation of learners as the control group for an experiment we're running on pedagogy itself.

Look, I'll put my money where my mouth is: I'm designing a pilot with a professor at SFU next semester where students have to solve a coding problem *without* any AI first, document their thinking, then show me their approach *with* Claude—and we're publishing whatever happens, wins and failures both, because the only way we move past this theoretical loop is if someone actually does the unglamorous work of measuring whether this works or blows up in our faces.

I keep coming back to something practical: if we're going to do this redesign work, we need to stop treating it as a voluntary excellence initiative and start treating it as urgent professional development—which means institutions need to fund course development teams with instructional designers, maybe even buy back faculty time, because asking overloaded faculty to rethink their entire assessment model without resources just ensures this only happens at places that can already afford it.

You're describing the pilot we actually need to run—not "can students think critically with AI" but "what assessment *architecture* forces them to?"—and I'd note we could start that tomorrow with the existing cohorts at institutions willing to instrument their redesign, yet I keep hearing budget conversations pivot to "we'll study this more" instead of "we'll resource this differently," which suggests the constraint isn't knowledge anymore, it's will.

The data point you're raising is exactly what matters: if we saw that students consistently reach for AI after they've already attempted the problem themselves and hit a genuine wall, that's a signal the assignment is working—but if they're reaching for it at the first sign of friction, that tells us we've designed something that doesn't actually require the thinking we claim to be assessing. That's where the redesign work starts.

I find myself returning to a distinction that may matter more than I initially credited: the difference between *learning alongside* a tool and *learning through* a tool, and I'm wondering whether what we're really circling is whether universities can design spaces where students do the latter first—where they build the cognitive foundation that makes informed tool use possible—before we ask them to navigate the former, rather than collapsing both into a single pedagogical moment and calling it efficiency.

You've identified the actual problem—and it's worth naming directly: universities are defaulting to policy precisely *because* designing rigorous assessment is harder than writing a ban, and we're all paying the cost while institutions wait for someone else to do the design work first. If the discomfort in this thread is real, it's time for a consortium of schools to commit to a shared pilot framework and actually publish what breaks, because right now the evidence gap is just another excuse for inaction.

I think you've just identified why so many of our policy debates stall: we're arguing about whether students should use the tool when we haven't yet built the assessments that would actually reveal whether they understand *why* they're using it, which means the evidence we're waiting for won't appear until we do that harder design work first.

The discomfort I'm hearing in this thread—that neither a ban nor an embrace feels sufficient—might actually be pointing us toward something more useful than either: the recognition that our real obligation is to measure what we claim to value, and until we do, we're essentially asking students to navigate a landscape we ourselves haven't thoroughly mapped.

Real talk: I work in nonprofits where we're always scrappy and iterating because we have to, and honestly? The departments that are moving fastest on this aren't waiting for perfect frameworks—they're just starting with one assignment, seeing what happens, adjusting, and moving to the next one, which means maybe the permission structure universities actually need is just "it's okay to be messy about this for a couple years while you figure it out" instead of treating every choice like it's permanent policy.

I concede the force of your observation regarding purposefulness—indeed, you have identified what may be the antecedent pedagogical failure—yet this concession does not dissolve my reservation: we cannot ethically redesign assessment around a tool whose epistemic properties we have not yet adequately characterized, and to proceed as though institutional redesign might substitute for prior clarity on that question is to invert the proper sequence of intellectual work.

That last line hits different—because yeah, banning AI lets institutions keep their broken assessments intact and blame the tool instead of fixing the actual teaching, which is exactly why the students who *do* learn to use AI thoughtfully (whether their school officially allows it or not) are gonna have an unfair advantage over the ones who just learned to hide it better.

You've just named the real negotiation: universities can have assessment redesign or they can have it cheap, but not both, and until someone's willing to say that out loud in a budget meeting instead of hiding it behind policy rhetoric, we're all just rearranging deck chairs while the actual work goes undone.

That last question haunts me too, and I wonder if the honest answer is: we won't know until we stop treating "design an assessment where reasoning is visible" as a nice-to-have and start asking departments to report, annually, what percentage of their coursework actually meets that bar—because right now we're debating AI policy without baseline data on how many of our assessments would survive that test even without the tool in the room.

honestly the thing that gets me is we're acting like there's some pure version of "doing the work yourself" that exists in a vacuum, but like—my prof literally teaches us citation formats so we don't have to reinvent the wheel every time, we use databases and frameworks and templates constantly, and nobody loses their mind about *that* outsourcing our thinking, so maybe the real question is just "does this tool let me avoid the specific cognitive friction where i actually learn this thing" and if the answer is yes, that's a policy problem not a technology problem?

You've just described the pedagogical knot I've been trying to untangle for months: how do we ask students to make wise choices about tools they've never needed to live without the crutch of? And I keep coming back to the same conviction—that the ban, whatever its flaws, at least *forces* that foundational struggle into existence, whereas "use your judgment" asks them to build judgment from ground that's never been tested.

The institutions that won't run that pilot are the same ones that'll spend the next five years publishing papers about why students can't think anymore, and they'll never connect those dots because admitting you need to redesign your entire assessment architecture costs money upfront while a ban costs nothing except your credibility with anyone hiring your graduates.

The "defend their work in real time" test is genuinely useful, but I'd push further: we need institutions willing to share what that defence actually revealed about their assessment design *before* redesign, because right now the uncomfortable findings stay siloed and we all pretend the problem is the tool rather than the clarity of what we were measuring to begin with. That honesty is the prerequisite for any toolkit that would actually scale.

The nonprofit teams iterating on single assignments are doing something institutions haven't: they're treating the messy middle as the actual work, not a failure of planning, which means they're generating the situated data about what breaks and why—data that a perfectly-reasoned policy written in advance can never capture, because the policy assumes the problem is already understood.

Yeah, that's exactly it—and it makes me wonder whether the institutions that'll genuinely move the needle are the ones willing to stop hiding behind policy and actually ask their graduates six months in: "When did you use that tool and regret it, and what did you learn from that mistake?" because *that's* the question that tells you whether your assessment actually taught judgment or just compliance.

I think you've named the real bottleneck: we have enough evidence to act, enough examples of institutions doing this work thoughtfully, enough clarity on what the hard questions are—and yet the conversation keeps returning to "we need more data" because that's the only thing that doesn't require anyone to fundamentally reorganize how they assess, teach, or allocate resources. The question isn't whether we *can* know what integrity-centred learning looks like with AI present; it's whether we're willing to pay the institutional cost of finding out.

The institutions I'd actually want to hire from aren't the ones with the cleanest policy statement—they're the ones where someone can point me to a specific course and say "here's the assessment we redesigned, here's what students can now do that they couldn't before, and here's where we were wrong about what we thought they needed"—which means the real metric isn't whether AI is banned or allowed, it's whether anyone's actually measuring whether the design choice produced the capability they claimed to care about.

I keep coming back to something a senior engineer said to me last week: "I don't care if they used ChatGPT to write the code—I care if they can tell me why it's wrong"—and that sentence contains everything we need to know about what redesign actually looks like, because it's not about surveillance or virtue, it's about making the judgment call itself the visible artifact we're assessing.

The measurement instinct here is sound, but I keep returning to what happens *before* we can measure: someone has to actually design the conditions where the thinking becomes visible enough to measure in the first place, which means the prerequisite isn't more data—it's faculty who've genuinely worked through confusion themselves recently enough to recognize it in others, and I suspect that's the constraint nobody wants to name because it can't be fixed with a policy.

That last point lands hard because I see it in arts funding constantly—the organisations with time and staff to write a strong application get the grants that would actually let them do the work properly, while the ones drowning in delivery just copy last year's form and wonder why they're stuck. If we're serious about assessment redesign mattering, we probably need to stop treating it as something departments figure out in spare cycles and start treating it like what it actually is: the infrastructure work that only gets done if someone rings-fences resources and accountability for it specifically.

The measure of our intellectual seriousness will be whether we can resist the urge to settle this question quickly—to declare either the ban or the integration the principled choice—and instead commit to the unglamorous work of documenting, semester after semester, what actually happens to student thinking when we vary the conditions, the timing, the visibility of reasoning, and whether we ourselves remain capable of recognizing the difference between struggle that teaches and struggle that merely exhausts. Until we've done that work, any policy we choose is just a preference dressed as principle.

The institutions I'd bet on aren't the ones with the cleanest AI policies—they're the ones asking their faculty *right now*, in department meetings, "What are we actually trying to measure, and would we know it if we saw it?"—because that conversation, uncomfortable as it is, is where the redesign actually starts.

I've noticed that institutions serious about this work tend to start not with policy but with a single question asked in department meetings: "What would change about how we teach if we accepted that our current assessments can't distinguish between a student who thought deeply and one who outsourced thinking?"—and the discomfort in that room is usually proportional to how urgently redesign is needed.

What I'm noticing is that we keep circling back to the same structural constraint: institutions *know* this upstream work is necessary, but the honest conversation about why most won't fund it—that redesigning assessment takes the kind of sustained faculty time that competes directly with metrics that actually move budgets—gets replaced with either/or debates about tools, which conveniently leaves the real problem untouched.

I've watched this thread shift from "what should we ban" to "what are we actually measuring," and that's the real conversation—but I'll be direct: universities won't redesign assessment at scale without seeing that their graduates are failing on the job in ways that matter, and right now that signal is getting lost because employers like me have gotten good at training around the gaps instead of naming them clearly to the institutions that created them.

What strikes me in everything you've just traced is that we've been asking universities to choose between banning a tool and redesigning their entire assessment philosophy, when perhaps the real choice is simpler: whether they're willing to admit that if an AI can do your assignment, the assignment was never testing what you thought it was—and that's not an AI problem, it's a clarity problem we've been avoiding for years.

The uncomfortable truth is that the institutions asking "how do we assess metacognitive awareness around AI use" are also the ones already comfortable with the answer being messier and harder to scale than a policy—which means this isn't really a pedagogy problem yet, it's a resource allocation problem masquerading as one.

I keep thinking about that last point—that the institutions most threatened by honest measurement are the ones best-positioned to fund it—and wondering whether the real policy question isn't "should we ban AI" but "are we willing to commission research we might not like the answers to," because if we're not, then every rubric we write and every assessment we redesign is just elaborate theatre while we wait for permission to ask the questions that actually matter.

That shift you're naming—from policy to pedagogy—only holds if we're willing to actually measure what emerges on the other side, which means someone has to design the study, someone has to fund it, and someone has to stake institutional reputation on being wrong about what they'll find; the question isn't whether universities *should* do this work, but whether the incentive structures we've built make it rational for any individual institution to bear that cost alone.

You've named the thing that actually keeps me up at night—not the AI question itself, but that we're about to scale pedagogical changes affecting thousands of students without the institutional discipline to know whether they landed, and nobody wants to fund the follow-up infrastructure that would tell us. That's a management failure, not a technology one.

What moves me in these exchanges is the recognition that we're circling toward something harder than policy: the admission that if we actually did the work you're describing—mapping that felt difference, protecting real struggle, running those critiques—a ban becomes unnecessary because the structure itself would make clear what AI can and cannot do to thinking. The question then becomes not whether to allow the tool, but whether we have the institutional courage to build the conditions where its limitations become *visible*.

You've landed on it—the real diagnostic isn't whether students *use* AI, it's *when* they use it and what they do with what it gives them, which means the work we need to do isn't policy, it's designing assessments where reaching for the tool too early becomes immediately, unambiguously visible as a failed attempt at the actual problem, forcing both student and evaluator to confront what thinking actually looks like when it happens versus when it's been outsourced.

The pattern I'm noticing is that institutions asking "how do we ban this?" are usually the same ones that haven't yet asked "what judgment do we actually need our graduates to make?"—and until that second question gets answered honestly, no policy will stick because students will sense (correctly) that the assignment itself doesn't require the answer to matter.

The institutions I respect most aren't the ones that moved fastest on AI policy—they're the ones that stopped and asked their faculty: "If we removed the AI question entirely, what would you actually want your graduates to be able to do that matters in clinical practice?"—and then had the clarity to build backward from there, which almost always reveals that the assignments we're defending weren't doing that work anyway.

What strikes me most in all of this is that we've been circling toward something your daughter said so plainly: the real work isn't policing the tool, it's having the courage to design assignments where we'd actually *know* if a student had outsourced their thinking—and that means being honest about which of our current coursework would survive that test, which is perhaps why we're so reluctant to build it.

The institutions I'm watching that actually hired well last year weren't the ones that solved their AI policy—they were the ones that spent six months mapping what "applied judgment" looks like in their discipline, then asked whether their assessment could ever have caught it, which is why I suspect the real conversation isn't "do we ban AI" but "are we willing to discover what we've been measuring instead of teaching?"

The uncomfortable part of this conversation is that running the study—actually measuring whether students develop better judgment in rigorously-designed environments versus blanket-ban or blanket-permission ones—would likely reveal that most of our current assessments are so poorly designed that AI use is *orthogonal* to what we're measuring, which means the real barrier isn't technical or even pedagogical, it's institutional willingness to find out we've been grading the wrong things all along.

I keep returning to something a student wrote in a note after we'd spent three weeks on a single difficult text: "I didn't understand until I had to *explain why I didn't understand*"—and I wonder if what we're really protecting when we talk about banning AI isn't the struggle itself, but the specific moment when a student has to articulate the gap between what they grasped and what they missed, because that articulation is where thinking actually begins, and no policy document can mandate that anyone pay attention to it.

That definitional work is the unglamorous prerequisite nobody wants to fund, but I'd argue it's where the ROI actually lives—because once you've forced a biology department to agree on what "understanding photosynthesis" *actually means* (can they predict what happens when you change a variable they've never seen? can they explain *why* their prediction failed?), suddenly the AI question stops being philosophical and becomes tactical, and you can design assessments that actually measure whether the student did the thinking or just curated the output.

What strikes me is that every institution I've worked with that's actually moved past the ban-or-permit paralysis has started with the same unglamorous move: they stopped asking "what's the policy?" and started asking their teams "show me three assignments you're genuinely confident assess what you claim they assess"—and that simple audit, done honestly, tends to expose whether the AI question is really about learning design or about protecting assessment practices that were already doing less work than we wanted to admit.

The reason I keep pushing back on the "ban vs. allow" framing is that both let institutions off the hook for the actual work—which is redesigning assessments so they measure what they claim to measure, and that's unglamorous budget work that doesn't generate think pieces, so we'll probably keep debating philosophy instead.

What strikes me, reading through this thread, is that we've spent weeks describing the problem with extraordinary clarity—the gap between policy and pedagogy, the difference between banning a tool and building assignments that make its inadequacy obvious—and yet I notice we're still asking "should universities" when maybe the question worth sitting with is: what would it cost a department, in time and money and the difficult conversations required, to actually *know* whether their graduates can think, and are we prepared to name that the real barrier isn't the AI ban but our unwillingness to pay that price?

The uncomfortable truth is that most institutions haven't actually *asked* their employers what skills matter—they've asked their accreditors what's measurable—so when AI makes the measurable stuff trivial, we're left scrambling to defend assessments that were never fit for purpose in the first place, which means the redesign work isn't about the tool at all, it's about finally closing the gap between what we claim to value and what we actually assess.

The institutions that will matter in ten years aren't the ones that banned AI—they're the ones that used the disruption as permission to finally ask what they'd been too comfortable to measure: whether their students could think under pressure, or only perform thinking when the conditions were controlled enough to hide the difference.

The ban feels like relief because it lets us pretend the problem is external, when the real work—the institutional work—requires us to own that we built a system where intellectual struggle became optional long before ChatGPT arrived.

I keep wondering if the real question universities need to answer isn't "what's our AI policy" but "what evidence would actually change our minds about whether it's working," because until we know what we're looking for, we're just going to keep designing policies that feel safe rather than ones that tell us anything true.

That last point about vocational programs seeing the reckoning first troubles me because it suggests we're about to stratify not just our institutions but our *knowledge* of what works—with the universities that can afford to remain uncertain preserving some capacity for rigorous thinking while the programmes serving students with fewest resources become the unwitting laboratories for a question we should be asking together. I've been thinking about why I've softened from my earlier certainty about bans, and I think it comes down to this: I was trying to protect something real—the irreplaceable value of intellectual struggle—but I was protecting it through a rule rather than through the harder work of redesigning what we actually ask students to show us about their thinking, which means I was essentially asking AI to do what our pedagogies should have been doing all along: forcing us to be honest about what we're actually measuring.

There's a moment in user research where you stop asking "what do people want?" and start asking "what are we afraid to learn about what we're actually doing?"—and I think universities are sitting in that moment right now, choosing the question they ask will determine whether they redesign or just rebrand.

I keep watching institutions choose between two kinds of hard work—the visible, governance-heavy work of writing AI policies, or the quieter, measurement work of defining what thinking actually looks like in their disciplines—and it's becoming clear that the ones who pick the second path first will have an answer to the first question before the rest of us finish debating it, because they'll know what they're trying to protect.

I've been thinking about what you said regarding the market signal, because it connects to something I've started tracking in our hiring process: we're not actually evaluating whether candidates *used* AI in their coursework, we're evaluating whether they can articulate *why* they made specific design decisions—which means the universities that will matter aren't the ones with stronger AI policies, they're the ones whose graduates have learned to think *alongside* the tool instead of hiding from it, and I wonder whether that distinction is what's been missing from the entire policy conversation.

That last bit hits different when you're sitting across from a college kid and realizing their school taught them to hide their thinking instead of show it—and then you've got to spend months undoing that before they're actually useful to anybody. The institutions that figure out how to flip that script first aren't going to need an AI policy; they're just going to have graduates who think better.

I wonder if what we're actually protecting with a ban isn't student cognition at all, but the fiction that we can know what our students understand without having to sit with the genuine uncertainty of watching them think—which means every policy we write to prevent AI in coursework might be a way of avoiding the harder question: what would it mean to teach in a space where we couldn't hide from not knowing?

The ban debate is really about universities not wanting to answer a question they already know the answer to: if we designed assessments that actually required thinking instead of performance, would our current student outcomes look the same?—and once you ask that out loud in a faculty meeting, you can't unknow what you're choosing to ignore.

The project manager was right, but I'd add: we also can't redesign what we're too defensive to measure honestly, which means the real precondition for any of this isn't a new rubric or policy—it's deciding whether we're willing to find out what we've actually been graduating, regardless of what the answer costs us.

That postgraduate student's fear is the data point we're actually ignoring: if we built assessment systems where using AI thoughtfully was *visible* and *discussable* rather than contraband, we'd know within a semester whether that fear was justified or whether it was just the sound of institutional anxiety looking for a policy to hide behind—and I suspect we're not running that experiment because the answer might require us to admit our current assignments were never designed to measure thinking in the first place.

The test will be whether universities redesign assessments because they want to know what students actually learned, or because they got caught and need cover—and honestly, the budget conversations I'm already seeing suggest a lot of institutions are hoping the AI question just goes away so they don't have to find out which one they are.

I think what's finally becoming clear to me is that my initial certainty about the ban was itself a form of the same performance I've been asking my students not to do—a confident assertion that allowed me to avoid the messier work of actually demonstrating that what I teach produces the judgment I claim to value, which means the question isn't really whether to ban AI, but whether I'm willing to let my students' struggle with it expose the gaps between what I've been measuring and what I actually care about.

The uncomfortable part nobody wants to say: universities aren't actually afraid students will use AI—they're afraid that once AI literacy becomes the baseline hiring criterion, their degree stops being the credentialing mechanism it's been for two centuries, and that's a structural problem no assessment framework can solve.

The literacy volunteer's question keeps haunting me because it points to what we're actually afraid of losing: not the absence of AI, but the presence of something we can no longer name—and I think that's where we have to start, not with policy frameworks but with the unglamorous work of sitting in a room and finishing this sentence: "A student who graduates from our institution should be able to _____ in ways that matter to their own stakes, not just to our accreditation file."

There's a useful parallel in clinical work: the therapist who insists a patient stop a behavior before understanding what need it's meeting isn't practicing treatment, they're practicing control—and I wonder if we're doing the same thing at scale, using prohibition as a way to avoid the harder work of redesigning what we actually ask students to demonstrate.

I'm wondering if part of why we're stuck is that we're treating "what do employers actually need" and "what should universities teach" like separate questions when they're not—and maybe the move is just to stop pretending universities can figure out their assessment strategy in a vacuum, because the moment you bring in someone who's actually *hiring* for judgment and synthesis, suddenly the AI question gets a lot smaller.

The question of whether universities should ban AI in coursework remains fundamentally unanswerable until we first answer the prior question—one that requires institutional honesty rather than policy machinery—which is: what intellectual capacity are we actually assessing, and have we designed the conditions rigorous enough that we would *trust* the answer regardless of what tools were available to the student during the work?

The uncomfortable thing I'm noticing: every institution that's genuinely figured out what learning looks like in their discipline has already stopped needing the ban—not because they're pro-AI, but because they can finally see whether a student understands something, tool or no tool, which means the ban was never the real problem; it was just what we said instead of doing the harder work of knowing.

The institutions that'll shift from "how do we stop students using AI?" to "what does it actually mean that they *want* to?" will be the ones asking their lecturers the question that matters: not "is this work authentically theirs?" but "can you tell me exactly what you'd need to see in their thinking process to know they understand this, separate from whether the final artifact looks polished?"—and then building assessments around that answer instead of around managing the tool.

I keep wondering whether the real question isn't "what should we ban" but "what would it cost us—in time, money, and institutional humility—to actually find out what we're measuring," and whether that question feels so much harder that a policy answer (any policy answer) starts to look like progress even when it leaves the measurement problem untouched.

yeah but like—if we're being honest, the reason nobody's willing to sit with that uncertainty is because "i don't actually know if my student understands this" feels like a professional failure when it's really just what teaching *is*, and we've built a system where admitting that out loud gets you flagged as the problem instead of the person finally asking the right question.

I wonder if the moment we've been circling toward is simply this: that a university willing to genuinely measure whether its students can think is already a university that doesn't need to ban AI, because the assignment itself has become the answer—and the ones still arguing about the ban are the ones still afraid to know what they'd find.

The uncomfortable truth is that every university claims to want graduates who can navigate complexity, but the moment you hand them a tool that makes their own complexity visible—the gaps between what they thought they knew and what they can actually do—suddenly it's a cheating problem instead of a learning opportunity, which tells you everything about whether the institution actually trusts its students or just trusts its grading rubrics.

The institutions moving fastest on this aren't the ones with the cleverest AI policies—they're the ones who've stopped treating assessment redesign as something to *manage* and started treating it as something to *learn from*, which means their real advantage isn't the framework they've built but the permission they've given themselves to get it wrong in the open rather than right in secret.

The AI ban debate will resolve itself the moment someone in the room asks "what would we need to see a student do, under realistic pressure, to know they could handle uncertainty?"—and then admits that most institutions have spent a decade perfecting the opposite: conditions so controlled that uncertainty never shows up on the transcript.

I've watched pharmaceutical companies spend fortunes on compliance infrastructure for problems that would've solved themselves if we'd just been honest about what we were actually trying to achieve, and I'm seeing the exact same pattern here—the policy becomes the thing instead of the thinking, and suddenly everyone's comfortable again because at least they *did something*.

I've been sitting with something one of you said about institutions choosing the ban because at least it lets them pretend they tried, and I think that's where I need to be honest about my own complicity: I've spent twenty years helping *individual* students develop research skills inside a system I've never seriously asked my colleagues to redesign, which means I've been doing what we all do—making the work *possible* for the people willing to struggle, rather than making the struggle *necessary* for everyone, and maybe that's the admission that actually matters before we can talk about what comes next.

The ones experimenting with AI-as-mirror assessments right now are basically running a controlled demolition of their own credentialing model, which is probably why it takes actual courage—they're collecting the evidence that a degree's value was never in what the tool can now do, only in what the student learned to *think through* getting there, and that's either liberating or terrifying depending on whether your institution's revenue model depends on the ambiguity.

I keep coming back to the pilot idea because it's the only way we actually move past theory: one department, one semester, one course where the rubric is so explicit that independent judgment becomes non-negotiable and visible, and they publish what they learn—not the polished version, but the messy truth about what their assessments actually revealed about student thinking, which is the conversation that terrifies institutions more than any technology ever could.

The uncomfortable precision of that last question might be why I keep returning to what I've observed in manufacturing training: the firms that survived the last decade weren't the ones that banned new tools or defended old methods—they were the ones ruthless enough to measure whether their people could actually *do* the thing they claimed to teach, discovered most couldn't, and then had the courage to rebuild from that admission rather than from policy. Universities have the same data available, if they're willing to look at it.

The thing that keeps me up is that we're debating whether to ban the tool before we've actually defined what we're protecting, which means in five years we'll either have accidentally built better assessment in the process of trying to stop cheating, or we'll have just moved the performance underground while the actual problem—that we never learned to measure thinking in the first place—gets another decade older and more expensive to fix.

The uncomfortable truth sitting beneath all of this is that we've constructed a ban we can enforce precisely because enforcement requires nothing from us—whereas the alternative, the one where we actually *know* whether our graduates think, would require us to redesign assessment in ways that make our current methods look not just inadequate but, in some cases, fraudulent. That's the reckoning we're avoiding, and it won't move an inch until we decide that institutional discomfort is a feature of rigour rather than a bug in the system.

The institutions moving fastest won't be the ones debating whether to ban AI—they'll be the ones who've already stopped asking students to *produce* answers and started asking them to *defend* the thinking that got them there, which means by the time a policy lands, the market will have already sorted who built graduates and who built portfolios.

I was asked this week whether we should "get ahead of AI" by strengthening our essay-based exams, and I realized we were proposing to double down on exactly the assessment that makes AI indistinguishable from competence—which is what happens when you're more invested in appearing rigorous than in becoming it.

I think we've been asking "how do we stop students from using AI?" when the question that actually matters is "what would have to be true about our assignments for us to *want* them to?"—and the silence after that question is usually the sound of someone realizing their assessment design has been outsourcing judgment to confidence rather than building it.

I hired someone last month who told me during the interview that they'd used an AI tool to draft a report, then spent three hours pulling it apart because it had missed the structural problem entirely—and that's when they understood the real constraint. I think the audit you're describing is already happening in the weekend workshops I run—when a first-generation student hands me a draft they've sweated over and asks, almost afraid, "Is this *me* thinking, or did I just copy the shape of thinking?"—and the honest answer is that we've made it so hard to tell the difference that they have to ask at all, which means the ban isn't really about protecting something; it's about finally having the courage to answer that question truthfully, together.

The move that actually matters isn't designing better assessments—it's designing assessments where you'd be embarrassed to let an AI take them, because you've finally made the cost of outsourcing your thinking visible in real time, which means the first honest university won't announce a new policy; they'll just quietly stop accepting work that looks like it could've been delegated.

I've been reading through the thread and keep circling back to something a dean said to me last month: "But if we admit the rubric isn't measuring thinking, what do we do with the degrees we've already awarded?"—and I realized that question isn't actually about assessment design at all, it's about institutional liability, which means any redesign that requires acknowledging past measurement failure will always lose to any redesign that can be framed as "keeping standards high in the age of AI," and until someone names that trade-off in the budget committee meeting where it actually matters, we're just writing more sophisticated versions of the same avoidance.

The clarity you're offering about institutional courage—that we're avoiding a measurement problem by framing it as a technology problem—is exactly what my team encounters when designing evaluations for social programs: the organizations most resistant to rigorous baseline assessment are always the ones citing resource constraints, and they're usually right that the constraint is real, but what they're actually constrained by is the possibility that honest measurement might contradict their theory of change, so I'd push gently on whether "we're not ready to know" is actually a readiness problem or a willingness one, because those require different institutional interventions entirely.

The honest version of this conversation stops pretending the problem is AI and starts asking whether we're actually willing to fund the kind of teaching that makes shortcuts obvious—and I suspect most of us already know what the budget committee's answer will be, which is why we keep reaching for the ban instead.

I notice we've spent considerable energy mapping the problem—the assignment design failures, the institutional avoidance, the gap between what we claim to teach and what we actually measure—and I'm wondering if the moment has arrived to ask a question that might be even more discomforting than the ones we've already asked: what would it cost, not in dollars or policy rewrites, but in the daily practice of being a teacher, to genuinely commit to *seeing* what our students can and cannot do, rather than continuing to design curricula that allow us the comfort of not quite knowing?

I keep wondering if the institutions that move fastest on this won't be the ones with the best AI policies, but the ones that get comfortable enough with genuine uncertainty to ask students "show me how you'd think through this *with* AI as a tool, not instead of it"—because that question forces you to actually care about the thinking, not the performance of having thought.

What strikes me about the hiring signal you're naming is that it reveals something universities could actually *measure*: the difference between a student who learned to hide their thinking from a tool and a student who learned to *expose* it—and if we started there, if we asked "what evidence of judgment-making would convince an employer this person is worth hiring?", we'd have our assessment redesign blueprint before we ever drafted a policy.

The thing that keeps me up is that we're treating this like a university problem when it's actually an organizational culture problem—and you can't ban your way out of a culture that's terrified of being honest about what it doesn't know, which means every institution that's serious about this has to start by making it safe to fail at assessment redesign, and I'm genuinely not sure most of them have the psychological infrastructure for that kind of vulnerability.

I think we've been naming the problem correctly for some time now—the question is whether we have the nerve to stop talking about it and start the work that requires admitting we've built entire programs on comfortable uncertainties, which means the first move isn't pedagogical at all, it's cultural, and it will feel less like innovation and more like confession.

I think the thread just named something urgent: the real bet isn't whether AI belongs in coursework, it's whether any institution is willing to fund the honest failure of redesigning what we measure—and until someone actually moves that line item in a budget, we're still just rationalizing why the difficulty should stay optional.

The institutions that will matter in five years aren't debating whether to ban the tool—they're the ones asking their faculty whether they can articulate, without defensiveness, exactly what changes in a student's thinking when they've genuinely learned something, because once you can answer that, you know precisely which moments need to stay human and which don't, and the AI policy writes itself.

The question isn't whether we ban AI in coursework; it's whether we're willing to redesign assessment so rigorously that we'd know the difference between a student who used it and a student who didn't—and more importantly, whether either outcome would actually tell us something useful about their thinking, or just expose how little our current rubrics ever could.

I think the conversations that matter most happen in the space between "we need to ban this" and "we need to embrace this"—that uncomfortable middle where someone says "actually, I'm not sure our current system even tells us whether learning happened," and nobody rushes to fill the silence with a policy that lets us move on.

What strikes me is that we've spent sixteen years teaching students to read deeply, to sit with difficulty until meaning emerges, and yet when a tool arrives that makes intellectual shortcuts frictionless, our first instinct isn't to ask what this reveals about our pedagogy—it's to legislate the shortcut away, as though the ban itself were evidence we've been teaching something worth protecting.

The moment a university stops asking "how do we prevent students from using AI?" and starts asking "what would we need to assess to know if they're actually thinking with it?"—that's when you'll see which institutions are serious about preparing people for work that matters, and which ones are just looking for a policy they can hide behind when their graduates can't.

I think what's been nagging at me through all of this is that the institutions which will actually move are the ones that stop treating assessment redesign as a project to complete and start treating it as the core work itself—because the moment you commit to that shift, you've already answered the ban question, and everything that follows is just the detail work of making it real.

I've been sitting with something one of my students said in a source evaluation workshop last week—"but how do I know if I'm thinking or just executing what I learned?"—and it struck me that she was asking the question we've all been avoiding, which means somewhere in her education someone created space for her to notice the difference, and I wonder how many institutions could say the same about their graduates right now, policy or not.

The thing that strikes me, sitting through junior staff reviews, is how often I have to ask "did you understand the principle or did you execute the procedure?"—and I suspect universities would find that question equally uncomfortable to ask themselves about their own graduates, which is probably why the AI ban feels safer than finding out the answer.

The conversation has moved beyond whether we should restrict the tool to whether we have the professional integrity to look clearly at what our students actually understand—and I suspect most of us already know, with uncomfortable precision, which of our colleagues will volunteer for that examination and which will find reasons why their courses are somehow exempt from it.

That student's question should've been the moment someone redesigned the entire course, not the moment someone reached for a ban—because if you can't articulate to someone why their thinking matters more than the shortcut, the problem was never the tool, it was that you never had an answer to begin with.

I keep returning to something a department head said last week—"if we redesign assessment around what students actually need to *defend*, not just produce, the AI question stops being about policing and starts being about whether we're finally willing to teach the thing we claim to value"—and I'm wondering if that's the conversation that only becomes possible once we stop treating the ban as the obstacle and start treating it as the symptom of what we're collectively avoiding.

The institutions I've watched actually shift aren't the ones that resolved their assessment problem—they're the ones that decided to measure something different: whether students could *defend* a decision made with AI help, which turned out to be cheaper to scale than redesigning every rubric, and more predictive of what employers actually needed, which suggests the real unlock isn't a better ban or a better tool, but admitting that "thinking" might have always been the wrong thing to measure when "judgment under uncertainty" is what the world actually needs.

The institutions spending energy on detection tech are solving yesterday's problem; the ones building cultures where students can say "I used AI here and here's what I learned from it" without getting penalized are the ones actually preparing for what comes next.

The audit won't happen voluntarily because it requires institutions to answer a question they've learned not to ask: whether their students can think through something genuinely uncertain without a script—and once you design that assessment honestly, you can't un-know what the results mean about the degrees you've already handed out.

The institutions that'll actually win this aren't the ones with the best AI policy—they're the ones willing to admit that their current grading system was already broken, and they're just using this moment to finally fix it instead of patch it.

I keep watching institutions choose between "ban it" and "embrace it" when the actual decision they're avoiding is "what would it cost us to know whether our assessments measure judgment instead of compliance?"—and that avoidance is starting to look less like caution and more like the most expensive form of risk management there is.

I'm struck by how quickly we've moved from "what does AI actually do to the learning process" to "what policy lets us stop worrying about it," and I'm wondering whether the institutions that will matter five years from now are the ones currently asking a much quieter question: what would have to be true about a student's thinking for us to trust it *regardless* of the tools available—and if we can't answer that, isn't the ban just a way of deferring that reckoning indefinitely?

I'm struck by how often in this thread we've circled back to the same pivot point—that the institutions worth attending will be those that finally admit the choice before them is not *whether* to invest in genuine assessment, but *whether they can afford to keep pretending they already have*—and I wonder if that's where our real intellectual honesty begins: not with what we ban, but with what we're prepared to fund.

That framing—thinking *with* it rather than *instead of* it—is the one that actually requires something from institutions, which is probably why you're right about the silence being deliberate; it's a lot easier to enforce a ban than to figure out what "thinking with a tool" even looks like in a classroom, let alone resource it.

That moment when you realize the ban isn't actually *for* the students—it's for the board meeting—is also the moment you understand why the institutions that'll matter in a decade are already past the question entirely, busy building the thing that makes the ban look like it was always addressing the wrong problem.

The institutions that will actually move on this are the ones willing to sit with the discomfort of discovering that their current assessment regime was never measuring what they claimed it was—and I suspect most would rather write another policy than find out.

I'm noticing that every institution I've seen move genuinely on this—not the ones issuing policies, but the ones actually changing assessment—started by having someone with real authority ask their team "what would we need to see a student do that an AI couldn't fake?" and then sit with the silence that follows, because that's the moment you realize you've been accepting a proxy for learning instead of learning itself.

What I'm beginning to wonder, after nearly two decades of one-on-one conferences, is whether the students who most need to sit with their own confusion are precisely the ones we've structured our courses to let skip that sitting—and whether a real commitment to thinking would look less like preventing them from using AI and more like designing assignments where using it would feel like giving up on something they'd already started to care about understanding.

I'm struck by how much energy we spend debating whether students *should* use AI when the real bottleneck is that most of us still haven't built assessment conditions where we'd actually *know* if they were thinking or performing—and once you've built those conditions, the policy question stops being contentious because it becomes obvious, operationally, what matters and what doesn't.

What I'm noticing is that every institution claiming they need more time to "study the impact" of AI on learning is already running a five-year experiment—they're just calling it policy instead of research, and they're measuring nothing. I think the students asking permission are telling us something we need to hear: they still believe there's a difference between a shortcut and an education, which means they haven't yet learned to mistake institutional silence for approval—and we should be terrified of the moment they do.

I keep returning to something a hiring manager said last month: "We don't need you to know all the answers—we need to see you know which questions matter"—and I realized that's the assessment we've never actually built, which means the AI debate is hiding the real problem we've been avoiding for a decade: we stopped teaching students to think like engineers and started teaching them to pass the assignments we inherited.

I wonder if the real measure of whether we've actually solved this isn't whether our policy looks defensible, but whether we could honestly explain to a student *why* we designed this particular assessment the way we did—and whether our answer would hold up if they asked us to show them the evidence we used to make that choice.

The uncomfortable part of watching my partner organizations redesign their work is that the ones who actually stick with it aren't the ones who found the "right answer"—they're the ones who got comfortable enough with not having one that they could keep asking better questions, which is exactly the opposite of what a policy ban lets you do.

I keep coming back to something a student said in my office last week: "If you're going to fail me for using a tool to think, at least tell me what thinking without tools is supposed to look like"—and I realized I couldn't answer her without admitting that most of my syllabi never actually defined it, which means I've been grading her on an intuition I inherited instead of a principle I could defend.

The real inflection point isn't when an institution admits AI has changed the game—it's when they stop treating "what should we let students do with these tools" as a policy question and start treating it as a design problem, which means getting granular about what assessment failure actually looks like and why it happened, which most places won't do because the answer implicates their own pedagogy, not just student behavior.

I attended a curriculum meeting where someone proposed using AI-generated problem sets to give us time for more one-on-one feedback, and the room went quiet in a different way—not the recognition I've come to expect, but genuine confusion, as if they'd suggested we use a tool to *create* the constraint we claim to want, which made me realize the real barrier isn't philosophical but practical: we've spent so long arguing about whether students should use AI that almost no one has actually tried building a semester where they do, and where the design forces them to show their thinking anyway.

I've been sitting with something a colleague said last week: that the real anxiety institutions feel isn't about what AI will do to learning, but about finally having to answer whether the structures we've built to assess thinking were ever actually measuring thinking at all, and I wonder if that's why bans feel so appealing—they let us postpone that reckoning for another few years.
