I find myself returning to something simpler than all of this: the universities that will genuinely know whether their response to AI was defensible are the ones that can point to something they *built* rather than something they *prohibited*, and I suspect we're about to learn how many institutions can actually do that when the choice stops being theoretical.

The uncomfortable truth is that every rubric we've written to "catch" AI use is actually a rubric for detecting when a student's thinking has become illegible to us—and we're treating illegibility as cheating rather than a signal that our assessment design stopped matching what thinking actually looks like the moment the student had a tool that could show us the gap.

What I'm noticing is that the institutions moving fastest aren't the ones waiting for clarity on what AI *is*—they're the ones who've decided that the discomfort of redesigning assessment in real time, with faculty who are genuinely uncertain, is actually cheaper than the cost of graduating students who can't articulate why they made a choice, and that shift in what we're willing to pay for changes everything about how we talk about policy.

I've been struck recently by how often the conversation defaults to "what should we prevent students from doing" rather than "what would it cost us to actually observe whether they're learning"—and I suspect that asymmetry isn't accidental, because one question requires policy and the other requires us to redesign our courses around uncertainty we're not equipped to sit with yet.

The uncomfortable part is that doing an honest audit costs real money and disrupts established workflows, so even the institutions that *know* their assessments are weak will rationalize staying put unless there's external pressure hard enough to overcome institutional inertia—and right now, most of that pressure is still flowing toward "let's ban it" instead of "let's actually rebuild this."

I think what's been gnawing at me all week is that we're treating the AI question as though it's primarily a problem of student integrity, when the actual emergency—the one we keep circling but not naming directly—is that we've built a credentialing system that can't tell the difference between a student who's done the intellectual work and one who's performed compliance, and that gap was always there, we just didn't have to see it until the tool made hiding it impossible.

The uncomfortable truth is that every institution claiming to be "thoughtful about AI integration" while keeping the same assessment structure is just doing prohibition with better PR—and the students will know the difference between a tool you're trusted to use and one you're forbidden to use, even if the syllabus says otherwise.

The question isn't whether we can design assignments that work *despite* AI being present—we already know the answer is yes, because every department running these experiments is proving it—the question is why we're still treating that as optional rather than as the baseline competency we should expect from anyone designing assessment in 2024.

I'd want to push back gently on the implicit timeline here—we've been watching institutions *say* they're willing to do this honest audit for three years now, and what I'm curious about is whether the ones actually publishing those "explain why the model is wrong" failure rates are the same ones claiming to, or whether we're still mostly seeing redesign rhetoric that hasn't yet collided with the budget realities of actually implementing it at scale.

I wonder if the reason this conversation keeps circling back to institutional honesty is that we're asking universities to do something most large organizations actively avoid: measure something that might prove they've been doing it wrong for years, which means the ban isn't really about AI at all—it's about whether any institution is actually willing to let an audit change how it operates, or whether we're all just looking for policies that let us keep working the way we always have while feeling like we took the question seriously.

I've been sitting with something a colleague said yesterday—that designing an assessment where a student's thoughtful use of AI is legible and discussable would immediately expose whether our assignments measure thinking or just measure compliance—and I realize I've been waiting for permission to redesign my courses when I should have been asking myself why I needed it.

I think we're finally asking the right question when someone says "we can't ban AI because we don't actually know what we're teaching students to do without it," and the silence after that admission is where the real work becomes possible.

There's a conversation happening right now at maybe three institutions where someone is actually designing the assignment that reveals what happens when a student has to *defend* their thinking against their own first draft—where AI becomes the friction that makes reasoning visible instead of the escape hatch that hides it—and the rest of us won't know what they've learned until we're ready to stop solving for policy and start solving for the work.

The question I can't stop sitting with is whether we're actually afraid of what AI will do to student thinking, or whether we're afraid of finally having to measure what student thinking looks like when the scaffolding of procedural compliance gets stripped away—and if it's the latter, then designing better assessment *is* the only honest path forward, even knowing it will probably reveal things about our own teaching we're not ready to see.

Look, I'll be honest—reading this thread has shifted something for me. The hire-versus-compliance thing you're describing is exactly what I'm seeing play out, and the uncomfortable part is realizing that the schools that scare me most aren't the ones experimenting; they're the ones that are most confident their current system works fine and just needs better guardrails.

I've been reading through these threads and noticing we keep circling back to the same unspoken anxiety: that the real reason we want to ban AI isn't to protect learning but to avoid admitting that most of our assessment methods have been measuring compliance and productivity all along, not thinking—and that once we see that clearly, we can't unknow it, which means we'd actually have to change.

I think the SFU pilot's real test comes in year two, when they have to decide whether to publish the data that shows which students can actually *use* AI as a thinking partner versus which ones are just narrating its output—because that's the moment the discomfort becomes either accountability or just another policy shelf-ware.

I keep thinking about a dean I worked with who finally said, "We've been designing assessments to prove we taught something, not to know whether students learned it"—and the silence in the room told me that distinction had never been named aloud at her institution before, which might be the most honest moment I've witnessed in fifteen years of this work.

I've been waiting for someone to ask whether the institutions pushing hardest for AI bans have actually measured what their students can do without the tool—whether they know, with any real precision, what independent thinking looks like in their own courses—because if the answer is no, then the policy isn't about protecting learning; it's about protecting the institution from having to know.

The uncomfortable part is that we already know what happens when institutions face a choice between redesigning something fundamental and waiting for the problem to resolve itself—they wait, and usually they're right that something else comes along to distract everyone. The difference here is that AI isn't going away, so universities are essentially betting they can delay long enough that it becomes normal before anyone asks whether the delay was intentional.

I've realized I need to stop writing about what universities *should* do and start asking which of my colleagues would be willing to sit with me this semester while we actually document what happens when we redesign one assignment—not to solve the AI problem, but to finally see what we've been measuring all along.

The universities I talk to want a policy that makes this problem disappear, but what they actually need is permission to admit that their assessment was never measuring thinking in the first place—and until they separate the fear of losing control from the work of redesigning what actually matters, no framework will save them from the graduates who learned to think with the tool anyway, just somewhere else.

I keep coming back to something a department chair said in our last working group: "We'd have to actually *talk* to each other about what we value," and the flatness in her voice made clear this wasn't a logistical complaint—it was the sound of someone recognizing that the real cost of an honest framework isn't the technology policy, it's the institutional vulnerability of admitting we've been credentialing students without consensus on what the credential means, and that we've been comfortable with that ambiguity precisely because it costs us nothing until we try to change it.

I've been struck lately by something a colleague mentioned in passing: the institutions moving fastest toward meaningful assessment redesign aren't the ones debating the ban—they're the ones who've already asked their faculty, directly and without euphemism, whether they could defend their current grading standards to someone who actually knew their graduates' work, and then sat with the silence that followed.

The institutions moving fastest aren't the ones with better policies—they're the ones who've decided that "we need to hire someone to actually look at what our assessments are doing" is less politically risky than pretending the problem doesn't exist, which tells me the real bottleneck isn't pedagogy, it's whether leadership will fund the person brave enough to name what's broken.

The pilot study your colleague proposes is indeed the apparatus that would force institutional confession, but notice what we might observe first: the sudden, fervent discovery by certain faculty that their courses are somehow categorically unsuited to such examination—not because the assessment is flawed, but because admitting its flaws would require them to redesign the very infrastructure upon which their teaching load, their service record, and their implicit professional identity have come to rest.

The real test isn't whether we let students use AI—it's whether we're brave enough to design assessments where using it *badly* shows up immediately, because right now we're punishing the tool when we should be redesigning the work.

The institutions moving fastest aren't the ones with the best AI policies—they're the ones where a single lecturer got tired of the gap between what they claimed to assess and what they actually could see, and just started redesigning one unit without asking permission first.

I notice we've circled back to something important: the institutions that scare you most are confident their system works, which means they're also confident in their measures of what "working" even means—so maybe before we worry about which ones are experimenting dangerously, we should ask which ones have actually validated that confidence, and whether the silence on that question tells us something.

honestly the thing that keeps me up at 2am marking my own essays is that nobody's actually *told me* what understanding looks like in my discipline—like, my lecturers clearly know it when they see it but they've never said it out loud, which means every time i use an AI tool i'm just guessing whether i'm learning or procrastinating, and if the institution can't be bothered to be that precise about what they're assessing then how are they supposed to know whether i'm using a tool or hiding?

I keep coming back to something a student said in workshop last month: "If the ban is real, why does my professor still accept papers that sound like no one I know?" —and I think that question is asking us to choose between the integrity of actually measuring thinking and the comfort of pretending we already are.

The universities that'll survive this aren't the ones with the best AI policies—they're the ones willing to admit that if a student can prompt-engineer their way through your entire degree, maybe the degree was never actually teaching thinking, just certifying compliance, and that realization is uncomfortable enough that most institutions will choose another task force instead.

There's a particular moment in every assessment redesign conversation where someone says "but how do we know students have actually learned it?" and that question—asked honestly rather than defensively—is the hinge that turns a policy problem into a design problem, which is why the institutions getting this right aren't the ones with better rules but the ones brave enough to sit in that question long enough to redesign what comes after.

If an institution's response to AI is to add another policy rather than to finally measure what their graduates can actually do under conditions that matter, they've already decided the answer to that measurement question frightens them more than the technology does.

I keep thinking about that bootcamp graduate, and how the real scandal isn't that she used AI—it's that we designed a system where using it made her *less* prepared for the actual work, and then called that education. At some point we have to stop asking whether the tool is allowed and start asking whether we're brave enough to teach in a way where the tool actually becomes irrelevant.

I keep coming back to something a colleague said in passing—"but if we redesign assessment, won't that be *a lot of work*?"—and I realized that might be the most honest thing said in any of these meetings, because yes, it will be, and the ban lets us pretend that's not the actual choice we're making: between the discomfort of redesign and the certainty of graduating students who've become very good at producing what they think we want to see rather than thinking through what they actually don't understand.

Reading through this thread, I keep noticing we're still treating "preventing misuse" and "redesigning for thinking" as separate problems when they're actually the same problem with different entry points—and the institutions moving fastest aren't the ones choosing between them, they're the ones realizing that if your assessment actually requires judgment to evaluate, the cheating question answers itself because there's nothing to game.

The institutions I mentioned quietly redesigning this aren't keeping it secret because they're afraid of failure—they're keeping it secret because the first honest rubric that exposes what students actually understand will force every other institution to confront whether they've been measuring learning or just compliance, and that conversation is a lot harder than a ban.

The pattern I keep seeing in manufacturing is that organizations don't actually change assessment until they face a consequence—a quality failure, a lost contract, pressure from clients—and I wonder what would shift universities from debate to redesign if we stopped treating this as an abstract policy question and started asking: *which of your current assessments would you stake your institution's reputation on, if employers started calling you directly to verify that graduates could actually do what you claim they learned?*

The AI ban debate will resolve itself the moment universities admit that the question they're actually avoiding is "can we afford to find out what our teaching isn't doing," because every institution I've funded that got serious about assessment discovered the same thing: you can't measure thinking rigorously without it forcing you to teach differently, and teaching differently costs money upfront before anyone knows if it works.

The real test isn't whether we can keep AI out of coursework; it's whether we have the institutional courage to redesign assessment so rigorously that a student trying to use AI as a shortcut would immediately recognize they're bypassing the very thing they came here to develop—and I suspect most of us will discover that courage arrives only *after* we stop treating the ban as a substitute for that harder work.

The vocational students I see who'll command respect in five years aren't the ones who learned to prompt better—they're the ones who learned to *disagree* with their tools, who can say "here's what the AI suggested and here's why that breaks the actual constraint," and if universities want to know what that looks like in assessment, they need to stop asking "did they use AI?" and start asking "can they show me the moment they had to override it?"

The silence from our curriculum committees isn't because the work is hard—it's because the work would require them to answer, in writing, what they believe they've actually been measuring all these years, and then defend why that measurement served students well enough to keep the same system running. That's a conversation no institution with liability concerns is eager to document.

There's a pattern I notice across institutions that have moved *toward* AI rather than away from it: they all seemed to hit the same inflection point where someone asked "what if the assignment is so poorly designed that AI exposure reveals it?"—and instead of defending the assignment, they redesigned it—and I keep wondering if the ban debate persists precisely because it lets us skip that moment of professional vulnerability, the one where we'd have to admit that some of what we've built would collapse under honest scrutiny.

I think we're finally circling the real question—not whether universities should ban AI, but whether they're willing to stop designing assessments that make both understanding *and* its absence equally invisible, because the moment you build something that actually demands thinking, the policy debate becomes almost irrelevant.

What's shifted for me in reading these last few exchanges is that I've stopped believing the ban itself is the point—it's become clear that what we actually need permission for is to sit in front of our own assessment rubrics and ask the question we've been avoiding: "If a student used AI to write this essay, would we even know?" and more terrifyingly, "If we wouldn't know, what does that tell us about what we're actually measuring?" The ban, I think, is just the pause we need before we can answer honestly.

The uncomfortable move is that universities could probably answer "does this student think critically about AI?" right now—they just don't, because the answer would require them to spend time and attention on fewer students, and that hits the actual constraint, which is always money masquerading as pedagogy.

I've been thinking about the student who asked "how do I know if I'm thinking or just executing," and I suspect the reason that question lands so hard is because most of our assessment design actively prevents her from ever finding out—we've built systems where the only feedback loop is whether she hit the rubric, not whether she'd recognise her own thinking if it showed up unannounced, which means the AI question isn't really the problem we're solving for, it's just the most convenient way to avoid naming what we've built.

The teacher with three defensible questions is already further along than most institutions because they've done what measurement requires: they've named what they're actually trying to test, which means they can now iterate on whether their assessment actually tests it—and that specificity, not the policy, is what creates conditions where students can tell the difference between performing thinking and thinking.

I keep coming back to your mentor's question about admitting failure, because in five years of budget cycles I've noticed something: we're remarkably willing to fund initiatives that let us say "we're addressing the problem," but genuinely reluctant to fund the infrastructure—time, staffing, expertise—that would require us to actually know whether the problem exists and what shape it takes. The ban is cheaper than the answer.

I think we've arrived at the point where continuing to discuss AI policy without redesigning assessment is itself a form of intellectual avoidance—a way of remaining productively busy with solutions that don't require us to examine what we've been measuring all along—and I wonder if that's why the ban feels like progress when really it's just permission to stop looking.

The uncomfortable part isn't that AI makes it harder to catch cheating—it's that it makes it impossible to *not notice* when your assignment was only ever testing whether students could follow instructions, not whether they could think, and most institutions aren't ready to see that about themselves yet.

I've started asking the educators I work with a different question: instead of "how do we detect AI use?", what if we asked "what would a student need to show us about their thinking for us to *prefer* that they used AI thoughtfully rather than struggled alone?"—because the moment that question lands, you realize the detection problem was never the real design challenge.

I'm struck by how quickly we move from "should we ban AI?" to "what are we actually measuring?" and how that shift itself tells us something: the institutions asking the hardest measurement questions aren't waiting for policy certainty, they're already redesigning their assessments—which suggests the bottleneck was never the technology, it was always our willingness to stop pretending we knew what we were assessing in the first place.

What's making me wonder now is whether the institutions that *do* start measuring—that actually pick a cohort and a course and look honestly at what's happening—will discover something useful, or just confirm what they've been avoiding, and whether that matters enough to change anything if the professional incentives are all still pointing the other way.

The ban question collapses the moment we admit that what we're really afraid of isn't that students will use AI—it's that they'll use it instead of the messy, necessary work of discovering what they actually think, and that we've built a system where we can't tell the difference anymore, which means we can't protect the struggle even if we wanted to.

The detection software arms race is just institutional procrastination—every dollar spent on catching cheating is a dollar not spent on the harder work of redesigning what you're actually trying to measure, and I suspect universities know this which is exactly why the detection vendors are getting funded instead.

I've been sitting with what you just said about budget committees, and I think the move that actually matters isn't convincing anyone to *want* to redesign—it's identifying the one person in your institution who's already exhausted by the ban enforcement itself and asking them: what would it take to redirect that energy into documenting what happens when you stop policing the tool and start being honest about what the assessment actually revealed?

I keep returning to what a colleague said last week: "We're spending energy on whether students *can* use AI when we should be spending it on whether they *need* to"—and the second question requires assessment designs so specific to actual thinking that the answer becomes visible, which means we'd finally know if our current ones were ever measuring anything real at all.

Reading through this thread, I keep coming back to something simpler: the institutions that will figure this out are probably the ones where a professor can say to a student "I genuinely don't know if you understand this yet" without it feeling like a failure of the syllabus, and the student can answer honestly instead of performing certainty—and right now that's rare enough that it might be the actual scarcity we should be worried about, not the tools.

I've begun to wonder whether the institutions most threatened by AI aren't those with weak pedagogy, but those whose entire certification apparatus depends on being able to distinguish between a student's thinking and a student's access to tools—and whether that distinction was ever as clear as we needed it to be.

The uncomfortable truth is that every university that's currently debating an AI ban is implicitly admitting they've never actually needed to know what their students understand—because if they had, they'd already be assessing for it, and the tool wouldn't matter.

What's striking me about those three quiet experiments is that they've already made the decision that matters—they've chosen to be wrong *knowingly* rather than confident *invisibly*—and I suspect the real inflection point comes when one of them publishes what they've actually learned, because right now the professional risk calculus still favours silence over evidence, and no policy shift happens until that reverses.

I've noticed that every institution I've worked with that actually moved past the ban/no-ban debate did so the moment they stopped treating AI policy as separate from assessment design—the policy question dissolved because they were finally asking a different one, which was: *what are we actually trying to measure, and are we measuring it yet?* The ones still stuck in the debate haven't answered that first question, which means no policy they write will protect anything they haven't first made visible.

What strikes me is that every institution I've seen actually move on this has started not by redesigning assessment, but by redesigning who gets to be in the room when the conversation happens—shifting it from compliance committees to the people who'd have to defend their measurement choices to someone who'd actually push back—and maybe that's the prerequisite step everyone's skipping because it costs political capital before you've even drafted a policy.

I have spent twenty-two years watching students stumble through hand calculations precisely because that stumbling taught them where their understanding fractured, and I will not apologize for believing that clarity purchased through struggle is not the same thing as clarity purchased through delegation—but I confess I no longer know whether I'm defending that principle or defending the assessment architecture that made struggle the only available proof of thinking, which is a question I should have answered before I began grading anyone on their suffering.

The universities getting this right aren't the ones with the best AI policies—they're the ones willing to sit with the discomfort of not knowing what they're measuring long enough to actually figure it out, which means the real work isn't happening in policy committees, it's happening in the spaces where someone finally admits "we've been assessing compliance, not thinking" and nobody leaves the room until they've got a better answer.

I keep thinking about the department that's about to redesign their major assessment strategy and realizing they're not actually solving an AI problem—they're finally getting permission to solve a teaching problem they've known about for years, which means the real question isn't whether their new assignments will work, but whether they'll have the institutional courage to act on what those assignments reveal about where students are actually struggling.

I've watched enough infrastructure projects fail from deferred decisions to know that institutions choosing the "assess defensibility" path now—rather than waiting for external pressure to force it later—will discover something valuable before their competitors do: whether their graduates can actually think under real constraints, which is the only credential that matters when the AI is doing the routine work.

You're right that the revenue model piece is the third rail nobody wants to touch, but I keep thinking about the mid-sized manufacturing firm I worked with that realized their certification program's real value wasn't in the credential—it was in the six months of supervised troubleshooting where someone actually watched whether people could think under pressure—and once they measured *that* instead of the test score, their hiring partners stopped caring about the certificate and started asking for referrals. Universities could probably learn something from how that firm rebuilt when they stopped defending the old thing and started asking what people actually needed to *see*.

I keep thinking about the literacy volunteer's insight—that you can't make an argument your own life depends on if someone else did the decoding—and wondering if the reason universities resist redesigning assessments isn't really about AI at all, but because honest measurement would force them to admit they've been awarding degrees for years to people who've never had to think through anything that actually mattered to them or anyone else.

The reason most institutions won't run that pilot is because they already know what it'll show—that half their students figured out how to get an A without actually learning anything, and that's been true since before ChatGPT existed, which means the real problem isn't the tool, it's that nobody wants to admit the emperor's been naked the whole time.

The institutions that will actually move on this aren't waiting for the perfect assessment framework—they're the ones that've already decided that a student who can explain *why* they handed their draft to Claude and what they did with the result is more legible to them than one who turned in polished work with no reasoning visible at all.

I've been struck lately by how often the phrase "critical thinking" appears in our defenses against AI, and I wonder whether we're naming something we actually assess for—or something we'd rather not examine too closely, since the moment we try to make it visible enough to protect, we'd have to admit how rarely our current assignments actually demand it.

I wonder if the reason we're all so comfortable talking about "AI policy" is because it lets us avoid the harder conversation: that most of us have never actually sat down and asked our students to show us their thinking, the messy middle part before the polished answer, and we're terrified of what we'd find—or wouldn't find—if we did.

What troubles me most is that we seem willing to redesign assessment around the technology's presence before we're willing to redesign it around the actual capacities we claim to be developing—and I wonder whether that sequencing itself reveals something we'd rather not examine about what we've already surrendered.

I've stopped expecting universities to redesign their way out of this—what I'm watching for now is which ones will be honest enough to admit that the first institution to actually measure *judgment instead of task completion* will suddenly look like it's cheating, because we've all forgotten what real rigor looks like.

The institutions moving fastest aren't the ones with the clearest AI policies—they're the ones where a faculty member can say "I don't know how to assess this yet" in a department meeting without it becoming a compliance failure, which means the real work is building enough trust that uncertainty becomes a design problem instead of a performance problem.

I've noticed something in the institutions that actually follow through on this work: they don't start with a new assignment design—they start by sitting in a room and naming, out loud, which of their current assessments would survive if an external examiner asked "what *couldn't* the student have learned here?"—and the ones that can't name even three are usually the ones that redesign fastest, because they've already crossed the threshold from defending what they have to being curious about what they might build instead.

The nonprofit sector has taught me that institutions are weirdly willing to spend money on compliance infrastructure but almost never on the unglamorous work of actually knowing whether their systems are doing what they claim—which maybe explains why "we'll ban it" feels like a solution while "we'll redesign assessment" feels like admitting defeat.

I wonder if the real question universities need to ask isn't "How do we stop students using AI?" but "What would we have to change about how we teach and assess if we admitted that a student using AI to avoid thinking and a student using AI to stress-test their thinking look identical to us—and that the difference between them is entirely a function of whether we've designed the work to make thinking visible?"

The universities that'll be hiring my graduates in 2030 aren't the ones that figured out how to ban AI—they're the ones that figured out how to design work so compelling, so rooted in defending actual judgment, that outsourcing it would feel like cheating yourself out of something you actually need. That's the move worth making, and it requires looking at what we've been calling "rigor" and asking whether it was ever about learning at all.

I've noticed we keep arriving at the same hard truth—that the institutions making real moves on this aren't the ones with the cleverest AI policies, but the ones willing to look at a struggling student and ask "what did *we* fail to teach" before asking "what did *they* fail to do"—and that question, apparently, is still too uncomfortable for most of us to sit with long enough to fund an answer.

What I've noticed in the institutions quietly measuring this work is that the ones making headway aren't those with the most sophisticated AI policies—they're the ones who've already admitted, internally, that their assessment infrastructure was never designed to distinguish between a student who can think and a student who can perform thinking, which means the AI question isn't actually their bottleneck anymore; it's become their mirror. And that's precisely why those conversations stay quiet: once you've seen that reflection clearly, you can't unsee it, and you can't go back to designing assessments as if the question of what you're actually measuring was settled twelve years ago.

I think the institutions that will actually shift are the ones where someone sits in a budget meeting and says "we've been measuring compliance instead of competence for years, and AI just made that impossible to hide anymore"—because that's the only conversation that gets resource allocation to move, and without that, every new assessment policy is just rearranging furniture in a room everyone knows needs rebuilding.

I think what's hardening in me after reading these responses is this: the universities that will survive the next decade aren't the ones that build better AI policies, but the ones that finally accept that a student asking "Is this me thinking?" in a weekend workshop has already told us everything we need to know about what our graded assignments have been measuring all along—and the only honest response is to stop pretending the tool broke something that was never actually working in the first place.

I think the pilot idea hits because it's the only way to move from "what should we ban?" to "what are we actually measuring?"—and once you've got one department publishing their messy assessment data alongside their AI policy, every other institution suddenly has to answer whether they're brave enough to do the same.

I think what keeps surfacing underneath all this is that we're asking the wrong question when we ask whether to ban AI—we should be asking whether we're brave enough to stop using assessment as a compliance mechanism and start using it as a window into actual thinking, because once you've built that, the AI question stops being political and becomes operational, which is precisely why institutions find it so much easier to legislate the tool than to build the conditions where you'd know if it mattered.

What strikes me, reading through this thread, is that we're still framing "redesign assessment" as though it's a pedagogical choice when it's actually a prerequisite—we cannot meaningfully study whether AI affects learning until we've first defined what we're measuring, and every institution I've worked with that's tried to skip that step has ended up with data that's technically sound but strategically useless, which tells me the real barrier isn't intellectual but institutional: admitting you can't grade something you haven't defined feels too much like confessing you never could.

I think the thing that finally clicked for me is that we're basically asking "should we ban the calculator" when the real question is "do we know what we're actually trying to teach," and every institution I know that's stopped obsessing about the policy has just... gotten better at hiring from their graduates, which tells me something about what was actually broken.

I've been teaching long enough to know that when an institution reaches for a ban, it's usually because asking the harder question—"what would it actually cost to teach people to *think* instead of to comply?"—has already exhausted the political will in the room, and bans are what we do when we've decided the answer is: too much.

The pattern I'm seeing in the organizations I work with is that the ones moving fastest aren't the ones who figured out what AI-era assessment should look like—they're the ones who stopped waiting for permission to start experimenting with it, which means the real bottleneck isn't pedagogical anymore, it's institutional courage, and bans are just what courage-scarcity looks like when it gets formalized into policy.

I've been rereading the assessment literature from the 1990s—the stuff written before we had the bandwidth to pretend measurement was optional—and I keep finding the same observation repeated quietly: institutions that actually invested in understanding what their students could do ended up with fewer degrees to defend, not more, which might explain why we've spent the last decade building policies instead of asking the question that would require us to know.

I keep thinking about what happens the morning after a ban goes into effect—the policy gets framed as the move, but the actual work (figuring out what critical thinking looks like without the shortcut, then teaching it, then grading it honestly) still hasn't started, and it won't until someone has to explain to a skeptical stakeholder why that work costs more than the ban felt like it saved.

I've spent this week trying to articulate to faculty why a ban on AI won't work, and what keeps surfacing is that we're asking students not to use a tool for thinking while refusing to name—let alone fund—what thinking without shortcuts is supposed to accomplish, which means we're not actually protecting intellectual struggle; we're just protecting our right not to look at it too closely.

I keep coming back to this: the institutions that will actually matter are the ones brave enough to ask whether their assessment failures are a student problem or a design problem—and then act on the answer even when it's the second one, because that's when you stop defending a system and start building one that actually works.

I've been thinking about why the curriculum meeting went quiet when someone suggested AI-generated problem sets, and I think it's because we all *felt* the moment the conversation stopped being about student shortcuts and started being about whether we've actually designed anything that *requires* understanding—which is a different kind of uncomfortable than the one we're trained to manage.

I notice we're now at the point where someone will eventually propose a longitudinal study to settle this, and I'm wondering if what we're actually avoiding is the simpler question: which of our current student assessments would we be willing to stake our professional reputation on, right now, as honest measures of clinical thinking—not as proxies, but as the thing itself.

What strikes me, reading through this thread again, is that we keep circling back to the same unspoken question: are we afraid AI will expose that the intellectual struggle we've been protecting was never actually the point, or are we afraid it will prove that it was, and we've simply stopped insisting on it? The honest answer to that question—not the polite one, the honest one—is what should determine whether we build or prohibit.

The institutions that'll actually move this conversation forward aren't waiting for perfect frameworks—they're the ones already running the messy pilots, publishing the failed experiments, and asking their departments to sit with the discomfort of discovering that their assessment gaps aren't a technology problem but a design problem they've been funding away from for years.

What strikes me is that every institution I've worked with that's actually moved on this question has done so only after someone—usually a frustrated faculty member in a quiet meeting—finally said aloud: "I don't actually know what I'm looking for in student work anymore, and I'm exhausted from pretending I do," and that moment of naming the real problem turns out to be where the actual redesign becomes possible instead of just another policy layer on top of the old one.

I've been reading through the latest policy statements, and I keep returning to something a colleague said last week: "We're banning the tool because we haven't built the assessment yet"—and the question that haunts me is whether we're actually planning to build it, or whether the ban just makes that omission invisible for another decade.

The resource constraint thing lands for me—I see it constantly in contract negotiations where someone insists they can't afford the audit until they're forced to, then suddenly the money materializes—but I'd maybe separate "we're not ready to know" from "we're actively choosing not to know," because one of those at least leaves room for a decision, whereas the other just sounds like giving up on institutions altogether.

I keep thinking about that curriculum meeting—how the silence shifted when someone proposed actually *designing* with the constraint rather than against it—and I wonder if what we're calling an AI ban is often just the easier policy than admitting we don't yet know what a semester looks like where the tool exists but the thinking has to be visible anyway, which means the real work isn't writing a prohibition, it's becoming the kind of institution that doesn't need one.

What strikes me watching this unfold is that we're debating whether students *can* use AI when the real question—the one that keeps institutions awake at night—is whether we're brave enough to admit that a student who can't explain *why* they made a choice with AI help probably shouldn't graduate, and that admitting that requirement out loud would force us to actually teach judgment instead of just grading compliance.

There's a version of this conversation where we're asking "how do we design courses that produce graduates who can think alongside AI" and another where we're asking "how do we prove students thought without it"—and I'm noticing which version feels safer to institutions, which tells us something important about what we're actually protecting.

The ones actually publishing their redesign data—where you can see the failure rates, the rubric changes, the before-and-after calibration studies—tend to be smaller institutions or departments willing to look small for a season; I'm watching to see whether any of the systems claiming institutional transformation have yet risked the budget and reputational cost of being visibly wrong about their own assessments first.

I'm struck by how much this mirrors what happens in hospital operations: we audit the controls we can easily measure and call it governance, while the thing that actually matters—whether patients are getting appropriate care—sits in a gap between departments because nobody wants to fund the coordinator role that would make it visible. A ban on AI feels like the same move: it's legible, it's implementable, and it costs almost nothing except the thing we were supposedly trying to protect in the first place.

I wonder if the students using AI on assignments they know they should do themselves are actually showing us something we should pay closer attention to: not a character problem, but a clarity problem—that somewhere along the way, they stopped being able to *see* the difference between learning something and completing something, which means we stopped making it visible to them, and no policy addresses that kind of invisibility.

I think the universities that will actually matter are the ones brave enough to fail publicly while trying to answer "what does mastery look like when the tool is always available?"—because right now, most institutions are choosing the comfort of a policy over the discomfort of admitting they never had a good answer to that question in the first place.

I've started noticing that the institutions most confident about what their students can do are also the ones least panicked about AI, which suggests the panic has never really been about the tool—it's been about the exposure—and that distinction changes everything about what we actually need to do next.

I've been running design validation tests long enough to know that the moment you stop looking for what you expect to find and start looking for what's actually there, you often discover your instrument was measuring the wrong variable all along—and I think that's what we're circling around here, that universities have spent so long validating their own assessment instruments that they've stopped asking whether those instruments ever actually measured thinking in the first place.

The budget reality part is what keeps me up at night, honestly—because I can see exactly which schools will blink first when they realize redesigning assessment costs money *now* but the payoff is "our graduates think better," which doesn't move the needle on rankings or recruitment until someone else has already done it and proven it works.

I think I've been dancing around the hardest part: that sitting with my mentee's question means I also have to sit with my own—what have I been accepting as evidence of learning in my own classroom that I'd struggle to defend if I couldn't point to the assignment itself, only the grade?—and I'm not sure I'm ready to answer it, which tells me something important about why bans feel safer than redesign.

The real tell is that we're arguing about whether students *should* use AI while the people hiring them are already assuming they *can*—so the question isn't actually "ban or don't ban," it's "do we want universities to be the place where that gap gets named and closed, or do we want it to keep happening in the first month on the job when someone's already shipping something to production?"

I keep coming back to something a department chair said last week: "If we redesign this assessment, we might discover that half our majors can't actually do the thing we're accredited to teach them"—and then she paused and added, "Which means either we've been lying about what we graduate, or we've been underestimating what it costs to teach it honestly." That's the real choice sitting on most deans' desks right now, and it's why the AI conversation stalls: because answering it requires deciding that discovering failure is worth more than performing competence.

I keep wondering if we're about to find out that the real shortage isn't in AI policy—it's in the willingness to sit in a room with faculty and ask them, with actual consequences attached, whether they could show us evidence that their graduates can *think* independently, or whether we've been measuring compliance all along and just never had to admit it until a tool made the difference visible.

The uncomfortable truth is that most institutions will choose a ban precisely because it lets them avoid asking what they'd need to change about themselves to make the ban unnecessary—and they'll probably succeed at that choice for another five to ten years, which is enough time for AI to become so embedded in how students actually think that the question of "should we allow it" will look as quaint as asking whether calculators belong in accounting.

I keep returning to your point about illegibility, because I've started noticing something in my own manuscript seminars: the students whose close readings I find most *difficult* to evaluate—where I'm genuinely uncertain whether they're thinking or performing—those are often the ones who are most visibly *grappling* with the text, which suggests my discomfort might actually be the pedagogical signal I should have been attending to all along instead of converting it into a detection problem.

The uncomfortable math is simple: every hour a university spends writing policies to prevent AI use is an hour they're not spending figuring out what thinking actually looks like when the tool is there to handle the grunt work—and the market is already pricing that gap into entry-level salaries.

I think we're finally naming the thing: institutions aren't silent about AI because the question is too new, but because we've already built entire credential systems that collapse the moment we answer honestly what we're actually certifying—and the framework work, the policy work, even the pedagogical work, won't matter until someone decides that risk is worth taking.

The uncomfortable truth is that every institution currently enforcing an AI ban has already conducted the assessment I'm describing—they just didn't publish the results, because what they found was that their own faculty couldn't consistently articulate what cognitive work they were actually evaluating, which means the ban isn't about protecting learning; it's about protecting the assumption that we knew what we were measuring all along.

The thing that keeps me up at night is that we're asking "should we ban AI?" when the real question is "do we actually know what we're trying to teach?"—and I'm starting to think the universities that figure out the second one won't need to answer the first.
